title: Kanban DnD column move: empty toast, no UI render, no persistence
description: In the perspective kanban/board view, dragging a card from column A to column B shows a toast notification with empty content (''), the card does not visually move in the UI, and the status change is not persisted on reload. Three failure modes: missing toast text, missing optimistic UI update, missing backend persist. Likely a single root cause in the DnD handler — verify the mutation is firing, the response is wired to store update, and the toast template receives the new status string.

title: forge-wasm: wa-sqlite OPFS binding + proof-of-concept query
description:
Successor to forge-f0gc (which delivered the crate scaffold). Wire wa-sqlite from Rust WASM via wasm-bindgen/web-sys, prove the round-trip with a simple query, and verify OPFS persistence across page reloads.

## Context

forge-f0gc landed the forge-wasm crate scaffold (Cargo.toml, ForgeRuntime skeleton, wasm-bindgen exports, native-test passing, wasm32 build verified, justfile recipes, CI gate). What it deliberately deferred — because it is a substantial standalone effort — was the actual wa-sqlite JS binding work. This bead picks that up.

wa-sqlite is normally consumed as a JS library that wraps SQLite-compiled-to-WASM with an OPFS VFS. From Rust we call into the wa-sqlite JS API via wasm-bindgen, which means: choose a wa-sqlite distribution channel (rhashimoto/wa-sqlite npm package vs CDN-hosted), define the Rust extern bindings, handle the async API (OPFS access is async-only and only fully supported in Web Workers in some browsers), and surface errors across the FFI boundary.

## Deliverables

- web-sys feature additions for OPFS (StorageManager, FileSystem APIs)
- wa-sqlite vendored or referenced via package.json (decision in this task)
- Rust extern bindings for the wa-sqlite JS API (open, exec, prepare, step, finalize, close)
- ForgeRuntime::open_database(path) async method
- Proof-of-concept: open DB → CREATE TABLE → INSERT → SELECT → assert result
- OPFS persistence verified manually (or via the test harness once it lands)

## Acceptance Criteria

- wa-sqlite opens a database from forge-wasm and runs a basic query
- OPFS persistence verified — data survives page reload (manual test acceptable; automated test depends on the test-harness successor)
- Errors from wa-sqlite surface as ForgeError variants
- Bundle size impact measured and documented
- docs/src/architecture/models/runtime.md updated with the wa-sqlite distribution decision

## Dependencies

- DEPENDS ON: forge-f0gc (crate scaffold) — DONE
- BLOCKS: forge-lu5s (BrowserStore adapter) — that adapter consumes this binding

## Worktree

.claude/worktrees/forge-wasm/ (shared with all forge-6z5l children)
close_reason:
Wired wa-sqlite from forge-wasm via wasm-bindgen, proved the round-trip end-to-end (open → CREATE → INSERT → SELECT → assert), and verified browser persistence across page reload manually in the harness.

Decisions: bundler-resolved npm (wa-sqlite@1.0.0 peerDep) — A unchanged. VFS swapped from OPFSAnyContextVFS to IDBBatchAtomicVFS because npm@1.0.0 doesn't ship a main-thread async OPFS VFS; functionally equivalent persistence; wasm-bindgen surface unchanged so Svelte/Tauri/Dioxus rewrite path unaffected. Worker scaffold still deferred (forge-73hi). ESM uses Asyncify build (wa-sqlite-async.mjs) per IDB VFS's handleAsync requirement. Error mapping: new `wasm` feature on forge-core gates ForgeError::WasmDatabase(String) sharing DATABASE_ERROR wire code with rusqlite variant.

Bundle size (release, wasm-opt): forge-wasm 28 KB→93 KB (+65 KB binding shim); wa-sqlite-async peer dep ~1.14 MB; net first-load ~1.3 MB cacheable.

Verification: forge-core 33/33 + 35/35 (wasm) + 33/33 (rusqlite) + 35/35 (both unified); forge-wasm native 4/4; wasm-pack --target bundler green; Vite production build green; rusqlite still excluded from wasm32 deps; manual browser round-trip + cross-reload persistence verified.

Successor beads filed: forge-n89p (P2, IDB→OPFS migration), forge-zf8i (P3, distribution channel revisit), forge-73hi (P3, AccessHandlePool/Worker upgrade). Pre-existing-issue beads filed alongside: forge-es6o, forge-2qns, forge-6t8v.

Manual PoC harness at crates/forge-wasm/examples/browser-smoke/ runs round-trip via Vite; will be automated by forge-901c.

Commit: 6dc9510 on branch forge-nst6 (pushed to origin).

title: forge-wasm: wasm-bindgen API + Svelte integration
description:
Define and export the coarse-grained wasm-bindgen API that Svelte calls. Integration test with Svelte app.

## API Surface
- init(config_json, snapshot_bytes) → ForgeRuntime handle
- query(sql, params_json) → JSON results
- extract_skills(text, entity_type) → SkillCandidate[] JSON
- align_resume(resume_id, jd_id) → AlignmentResult JSON
- search_skills(query, top_k) → SkillMatch[] JSON
- get_sync_ops(since_timestamp) → CrdtOp[] JSON
- apply_sync_ops(ops_json) → MergeResult JSON
- load_snapshot(bytes) → ()

Each call does substantial work in WASM and returns a complete result. No chatty calls.

## Svelte Integration
- Svelte imports forge-wasm via wasm-pack output
- ForgeRuntime initialized on app startup
- Svelte stores/services call WASM API methods
- Web Worker for extraction (non-blocking)

## Docs to update on completion

- [ ] docs/src/architecture/seams.md — Seam 4 (embedding space ↔ graph space) with actual FFI patterns

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All API methods exported and callable from JS
- [ ] JSON serialization overhead measured (< 1ms for typical payloads)
- [ ] Svelte app initializes ForgeRuntime on startup
- [ ] Extraction runs in Web Worker without blocking UI
- [ ] Full roundtrip test: Svelte → WASM → wa-sqlite → WASM → Svelte
- [ ] WASM bundle size measured and < 5MB (excluding embedding model)
- [ ] Docs updated
- [ ] Capture the work done in this session as a Memory, including what landed, key decisions encoded, lessons, and critical-path state. Save under /Users/adam/.claude/projects/-Users-adam-code-proj-forge/memory/ and update MEMORY.md index.

Mitigation — feature-flag the WASM consumer (e.g. VITE_USE_FORGE_WASM env) so the existing Hono API path keeps working. Land WASM-mode code ALONGSIDE the API-mode code; flip the default only after a full smoke pass. Do NOT delete the API-mode code paths during this task.

title: forge-wasm: extraction pipeline (4 extractors + RRF)
description:
Implement the 4-extractor RRF skill extraction pipeline in Rust within forge-wasm. This is the compute-heavy module that replaces LLM-based extraction.

## Deliverables
- Extractor 1: Embedding similarity (HNSW lookup, depends on graph runtime)
- Extractor 2: Dictionary/alias matcher (fuzzy string match against graph nodes)
- Extractor 3: Pattern extractor (regex for level/skill mentions)
- Extractor 4: Graph contextual (traverse graph from found skills, propose implied)
- RRF fusion: combine ranked lists from all extractors
- Entity-specific chunking strategies (JD, bullet, cert, free text)
- wasm-bindgen export: extract_skills(text, entity_type) → SkillCandidate[]

## Embedding model question
- Option A: Call transformers.js from Svelte, pass vectors to WASM for HNSW lookup
- Option B: ONNX runtime compiled into WASM for fully Rust-side embedding
- Decision needed: Option A is simpler (transformers.js is mature), Option B is more self-contained

## Docs to update on completion

- [ ] docs/src/architecture/seams.md — Seam 1 and 2 with actual trait definitions

title: Skill graph snapshot: serialization + distribution format
description:
Design and implement the snapshot format for distributing the global skill graph to browsers via CDN.

## Snapshot Contents
- metadata: {version, skill_count, edge_count, embedding_model, embedding_dim}
- nodes: packed array (id, canonical_name, aliases, category)
- edges: adjacency list (typed, with temporal metadata)
- embeddings: Float32Array (parallel to nodes array)
- hnsw_index: pre-built binary blob (usearch or hnswlib format)
- market_stats: skill_demand_stats aggregates

## Format
- Binary format for performance (MessagePack or custom) with JSON fallback for debugging
- Compression: gzip or brotli for CDN transfer
- Target: < 25MB at 10k skills

## Lifecycle
- Server builds snapshot (triggered by curation changes or on schedule)
- Upload to R2/CDN
- Browser checks version via ETag
- Download → IndexedDB cache → load into forge-wasm runtime

## Docs to update on completion
- [ ] docs/src/architecture/graphs/skills.md — snapshot format section with actual byte layout
- [ ] docs/src/architecture/models/runtime.md — snapshot lifecycle with actual sizes
- [ ] docs/src/architecture/models/deployment.md — CDN distribution details

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Serialization roundtrip preserves all data
- [ ] Size under 25MB at 10k simulated nodes
- [ ] HNSW index loads from snapshot without rebuild
- [ ] Version checking works (ETag-based)
- [ ] Market stats included in snapshot
- [ ] Docs updated
close_reason: Snapshot format landed: SkillGraphSnapshot + SnapshotHeader/Node/Edge/Metadata/MarketStat in forge-core, custom container layout (MAGIC + version + length-prefixed JSON header + raw embedding bytes + opaque HNSW blob) — keeps heavy binary out of JSON. encode/decode roundtrip verified, version + magic guards. build_structural_snapshot builder in forge-sdk reads nodes/edges in deterministic order. 6 forge-core snapshot tests (incl. 10k-node size budget under 25MB) + 2 builder tests. HNSW + market-stats payload sections deferred to forge-afyg/forge-c4i5 — format slots ready. skills.md, runtime.md, deployment.md updated.

title: Skill graph traversal API
description:
Typed traversal API for querying the skill graph. This is the contract that the extraction pipeline (Epic 2) and alignment scoring (Epic 3) depend on.

## API Surface
- find_aliases(skill_id) -> Vec<Skill>
- find_children(skill_id) -> Vec<Skill>
- find_parents(skill_id) -> Vec<Skill>
- find_related(skill_id, edge_types: Vec<EdgeType>) -> Vec<(Skill, EdgeType, weight)>
- n_hop_neighbors(skill_id, n: usize, edge_types: Option<Vec<EdgeType>>) -> Graph subgraph
- find_by_name(name: &str) -> Option<Skill> (exact + alias lookup)
- co_occurrence_stats(skill_id, time_window: Option<&str>) -> Vec<(Skill, weight)>

## Implementation
- For server: SQL queries against skill_graph_edges (recursive CTEs for multi-hop)
- For WASM: in-memory petgraph traversal (loaded from snapshot)
- Both implement same trait — SkillGraphTraversal

## Docs to update on completion
- [ ] docs/src/architecture/seams.md — Seam 1 contract details with actual trait definition
- [ ] docs/src/architecture/graphs/skills.md — traversal API section

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Trait defined with all methods
- [ ] SQL implementation working against skill_graph_edges
- [ ] Unit tests for each traversal method
- [ ] Multi-hop traversal tested (2-hop, 3-hop)
- [ ] Temporal co-occurrence query tested
- [ ] Docs updated
close_reason: SkillGraphTraversal trait + SqlSkillGraphStore landed. Trait in forge-core::types::skill_graph (object-safe, return Result<_,ForgeError>). 7 methods: find_aliases, find_children, find_parents, find_related, n_hop_neighbors (recursive CTE, undirected, optional edge-type filter via json_each), find_by_name (canonical + alias fallback via json_each), co_occurrence_stats (headline weight + windowed via json_extract). 11 store tests + 3 enum-roundtrip tests. seams.md Seam 1 + skills.md updated.

title: R1.5: forge-wasm — Browser WASM Data Layer
description:
Create the forge-wasm crate: the Rust WASM data layer that runs in the browser and survives every migration phase (Svelte→Tauri→Dioxus). This is the most important single deliverable in the browser-first architecture.

## What This Crate Is

forge-wasm is a Rust crate compiled to WebAssembly that provides the complete Forge data layer for browser execution. It consumes forge-core (types) and forge-sdk (repos/services), adds WASM-specific adapters, and exposes a clean API.

Svelte calls it via wasm-bindgen (MVP 2.0). Tauri calls it natively (R2). Dioxus calls it natively (R4). The expensive investment is done ONCE.

## What It Contains

- ForgeRuntime: init, config, mode selection (BrowserStore vs RemoteStore)
- BrowserStore adapter: wa-sqlite via wasm-bindgen/web-sys for OPFS
- Skill extraction pipeline: 4 extractors (embedding, dictionary, pattern, graph contextual) + RRF fusion
- Alignment scoring engine: graph-aware, typed-edge traversal, level matching
- HNSW index: usearch-rs compiled to WASM (or hnswlib-rs)
- Graph runtime: petgraph loaded from snapshot, in-memory traversal
- Embedding interface: ONNX runtime for WASM or thin wrapper around transformers.js
- Snapshot loader: deserialize global graph snapshot into runtime structures
- CRDT operation log: capture all mutations as CRDT ops for sync
- Encryption seam: interface for future zero-knowledge (passthrough impl for now)

## Relationship to Existing Crates

- CONSUMES: forge-core (types, enums — compile to WASM as-is)
- CONSUMES: forge-sdk (repos, services — need wa-sqlite adapter instead of rusqlite)
- DOES NOT CONSUME: forge-server (Axum routes are server-only)
- DOES NOT CONSUME: forge-ai (LLM client is server-only, replaced by local extraction pipeline)

## Architecture Position

This crate is the BOTTOM box in the architecture:

  Svelte (now) / Dioxus (R4) — UI layer, swappable
         |
    wasm-bindgen (Svelte) or native (Dioxus)
         |
  forge-wasm — data layer, built once, survives all migrations
         |
    wa-sqlite (OPFS) + HNSW + petgraph + CRDT log

## Why R1.5 (not R2 or later)

- R0 (DONE): forge-core types + forge-sdk repos/services exist
- R1 (in progress): Axum server — server-side Rust
- R1.5 (THIS): forge-wasm — browser-side Rust, consumes R0 output
- R2: Tauri — wraps forge-wasm + Svelte for desktop
- R3: MCP port — server-side, independent
- R4: Dioxus — replaces Svelte UI, consumes forge-wasm natively

forge-wasm MUST exist before R2 (Tauri needs it) and MUST exist before MVP 2.0 CF deployment. It is on the critical path.

## wasm-bindgen API Surface (what Svelte calls)

The API should be coarse-grained to minimize FFI boundary crossings:

- init(config, snapshot_bytes) -> ForgeRuntime
- query(sql, params) -> JSON results (batch DB operations)
- extract_skills(text, entity_type) -> SkillCandidate[] (full pipeline, one call)
- align_resume(resume_id, jd_id) -> AlignmentResult (full scoring, one call)
- search_skills(query, top_k) -> SkillMatch[] (HNSW + fuzzy, one call)
- get_sync_ops(since_timestamp) -> CrdtOp[] (for sync push)
- apply_sync_ops(ops) -> MergeResult (for sync pull)
- load_snapshot(bytes) -> () (update global graph)

Each call does substantial work in WASM and returns a complete result. No chatty per-field calls across the boundary.

## Dependencies

- DEPENDS ON: R0 forge-core + forge-sdk (types and repos — DONE)
- DEPENDS ON: forge-ucc2 (Skill Graph Data Model — schema must be stable)
- BLOCKS: forge-ae8y (Browser-Local Storage — BrowserStore IS part of forge-wasm)
- BLOCKS: forge-9is4 (Dual-Mode Adapter — mode logic IS part of forge-wasm)
- BLOCKS: forge-4a01 (MVP 2.0 CF Deployment — needs WASM bundle)
- BLOCKS: forge-of3p (R2 Tauri — Tauri backend consumes forge-wasm)

## Worktree Isolation

Suggested worktree: .claude/worktrees/forge-wasm
- New crate: crates/forge-wasm/

title: Hono API → Dual-Mode ELM Adapter

title: Skill Alignment & Matching
description:
Design and implement the graph-aware alignment scoring algorithm. Transforms binary 'have it / dont have it' into continuous scores accounting for transferable skills, hierarchy, and level gaps.

## Context

Current alignment is mostly direct skill name matching with some embedding similarity. This epic builds a scoring algorithm that traverses the skill graph to find matches at different relationship depths, adjusts for skill levels, and produces explanable alignment scores.

The Alignment Graph is COMPUTED, not stored — it forms ephemerally when you match a resume against a JD. But alignment results feed BACK into the persistent skill graph (co-occurrence statistics, relationship reinforcement).

## Scoring Algorithm

For each JD skill requirement, compute match score against all resume skills:

### Match Types (ordered by strength)
1. Direct match: resume has exact skill → 1.0
2. Alias match: resume has an alias → 1.0
3. Child match: resume has a more specific skill (child-of) → 0.9
4. Parent match: resume has a broader skill (parent-of) → 0.5
5. Sibling match: resume has a related alternative (related-to) → 0.4
6. Embedding proximity: nearest resume skill within threshold → 1 - distance
7. Co-occurrence: resume has skills that co-occur → 0.3
8. Cert validation: resume has a cert that validates this skill → 0.95 (authoritative)

### Level Adjustment
- Extract level from JD ('5 years', 'expert', 'familiar')
- Extract level from resume (bullets, annotations, cert validation level)
- Multiplier: exceeds=1.0, meets=1.0, partial=proportional, unknown=0.8

### Cert Integration
Certifications are HIGH-CONFIDENCE skill signals. CKAD certifying Kubernetes is stronger than a bullet mentioning Kubernetes. Cert matches should bypass normal scoring and contribute near-direct match confidence. This connects to the Certification Graph (future).

### Aggregate Scores
- Per-skill alignment = max(all match scores) * level_adjustment
- Overall resume alignment = weighted_mean(all JD skill alignments)
- Gap report: JD skills below threshold with match-type explanation
- Strength report: unmatched resume skills (transferable value)
- Coverage report: what % of JD requirements are covered at each confidence tier

## Seam: Alignment ↔ Skill Graph (Epic 1)

- Alignment traverses the graph using typed edges
- Edge types have numeric semantics (alias=1.0, child=0.9, etc.)
- Graph provides: typed traversal API
- Alignment does NOT modify the graph — but alignment events produce co-occurrence signals that feed back via Epic 4 (Curation)

## Seam: Alignment ↔ Extraction (Epic 2)

- Extraction produces the skill sets being compared
- Extraction quality directly bounds alignment quality
- If extraction misses a skill, alignment cant find the match
- Level signals from extraction feed into level adjustment

## Seam: Alignment ↔ Provenance Graph

The alignment answer isnt just 'you match at 0.85' — its 'you match because Bullet X from Role Y demonstrates Skill Z.' The provenance chain from skill → bullet → source → role → org is what makes alignment ACTIONABLE. This means alignment scoring needs to trace back through provenance to produce explanations.

## Seam: Alignment ↔ Domain/Archetype Graph (future)

Archetype context changes alignment semantics:
- A DevOps JD values Terraform higher than a Data Science JD
- Archetype → skill importance weights would make alignment context-aware
- For now: uniform weights. Future: archetype-weighted alignment.

## Seam: Alignment ↔ Career Target Graph (future)

Career Target is alignment at the AGGREGATE level — not 'this resume vs this JD' but 'all my experience vs this archetype.' Same scoring algorithm, different inputs (aggregate skill profile vs archetype expectations instead of resume vs JD).

## What This Enables

- 'You have Docker but they want Kubernetes' → partial match (sibling 0.4), not gap
- 'You have Container Orchestration, they want Kubernetes' → broad match (parent 0.5)
- 'You have Kubernetes, they want Container Orchestration' → strong match (child 0.9)
- 'You have CKAD cert' → authoritative match (0.95) for Kubernetes requirement
- Level gaps vs skill gaps distinguished clearly

## Dependencies

- DEPENDS ON: Epic 1 (Graph Data Model) — typed edges for scoring
- DEPENDS ON: Epic 2 (Extraction Pipeline) — extracted skills with levels
- Future: benefits from Domain/Archetype Graph, Career Target Graph, Cert Graph

## Worktree Isolation

Suggested worktree: .claude/worktrees/skill-alignment
- Touches: packages/core/src/services/alignment/, packages/core/src/services/skill-matching/
- Pure algorithm work — minimal file conflicts
- Safe to parallelize with: Epic 1 (after stable), Epic 4, UI, extension
- NOT safe to parallelize with: Epic 2 (shares skill matching interfaces)

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] All 8 match types implemented with configurable weights
- [ ] Level matching adjusts scores based on experience/expertise signals
- [ ] Cert matches produce near-direct confidence (0.95)
- [ ] Gap report with match-type explanations
- [ ] Strength report for unmatched resume skills
- [ ] Provenance trace: alignment result links back to specific bullets and sources
- [ ] Overall alignment score correlates with human judgment on 10+ test cases
- [ ] Score explanations available ('sibling match via related-to edge')
- [ ] Performance: < 100ms for typical resume vs JD
- [ ] Capture the work done in this session as a Memory, including what landed, key decisions encoded, lessons, and critical-path state. Save under /Users/adam/.claude/projects/-Users-adam-code-proj-forge/memory/ and update MEMORY.md index.

title: Skill Extraction Pipeline
description:
Design and implement the multi-extractor RRF pipeline for extracting skills from unstructured text (JDs, bullets, raw experience). Must work without LLM at inference time — targeting in-browser/CPU-only execution.

## Context

Current skill extraction is LLM-based (via MCP tools). This epic replaces that with a pipeline of lightweight extractors whose results are fused via RRF, enabling extraction in-browser, on CPU, or via SLM.

Skills are the JOIN TABLE of Forge — they connect JDs to bullets, bullets to certs, JDs to resumes. Extraction quality directly determines alignment quality, gap analysis accuracy, and resume relevance.

## Architecture: 4-Extractor RRF Pipeline

Each extractor runs in parallel on input text and produces a ranked list of candidate skills:

### Extractor 1: Embedding Similarity
- Embed input text chunks using small model (all-MiniLM-L6-v2, 384-dim)
- HNSW lookup against skill graph embeddings
- Catches: semantic matches, paraphrasing, related concepts
- Misses: exact acronyms, hierarchy levels

### Extractor 2: Dictionary/Alias Matcher
- Exact + fuzzy string match against all skill canonical names + aliases from graph
- Catches: abbreviations (K8s), exact mentions, misspellings
- Misses: paraphrasing, implied skills

### Extractor 3: Pattern Extractor
- Regex/grammar patterns for skill-level mentions
- Patterns: 'N years of X', 'experience with X', 'proficient in X', 'expert-level X'
- Catches: level/experience information, explicit mentions with context
- Misses: implied skills, mentions without level context

### Extractor 4: Graph Contextual
- Given skills found by extractors 1-3, traverse the skill graph
- If {Kubernetes, AWS} found → graph: K8s+AWS platform-for EKS → propose EKS
- If {Python, pandas} found → co-occurrence → propose data-science context
- Catches: implied skills, disambiguation, hierarchy completion
- Misses: cold start (needs other extractors to seed)

### RRF Fusion
- score(skill) = sum(1/(k + rank_i)) across all extractors
- k=60 (standard RRF constant), configurable threshold for minimum fused score

## Seam: Entity-Specific Extraction Strategies

The RRF framework is shared, but text chunking and pattern strategies differ by entity type:

### JD Extraction
- Pre-processing: section identification (Requirements, Preferences, Description, Benefits)
- Requirements sections get higher extraction weight
- Industry context from org (disambiguates 'Python at Goldman' vs 'Python at CrowdStrike')
- Level signals: 'required' vs 'preferred' vs 'nice to have'

### Bullet Extraction
- Single sentence, dense with implicit skills
- 'Managed production K8s clusters serving 10M requests/day' implies: Kubernetes, production ops, high-scale, SRE
- Only Kubernetes might be explicitly tagged — others are latent skills
- Semantic search over bullet embeddings finds bullets demonstrating a skill without explicit tags

### Certification Extraction
- High-confidence skill signals — a cert authoritatively validates skills
- CKAD → Kubernetes at practitioner level (bypass normal alignment scoring)
- Pattern: cert name → vendor lookup → validated skill list from Cert Graph

### Raw Experience / Free Text
- Least structured, most ambiguous
- Heavier reliance on embedding similarity and co-occurrence context
- May produce more candidates for human review

## Seam: Extraction Pipeline ↔ Skill Graph (Epic 1)

- Pipeline READS: HNSW index, alias lookup table, adjacency list
- Pipeline WRITES BACK: new skill candidates (HNSW misses), co-occurrence observations, confidence updates
- These writes feed into the Curation Workflow (Epic 4) review queue
- Contract: graph must expose typed traversal API

## Seam: Extraction ↔ Org/Industry Context

Org context changes extraction semantics:
- JD from Goldman Sachs (finance) → 'Python' = quant/data
- JD from CrowdStrike (security) → 'Python' = automation/tooling
- This context should feed into Extractor 4 (graph contextual) as a domain signal
- Requires: Org Graph (Graph 4) industry edges connected to Domain/Archetype Graph

## Seam: Embedding Space ↔ Graph Space

Embeddings give similarity. Graph gives structure. Python and JavaScript are close in embedding space but may have no graph edge. The RRF fusion resolves this — each signal gets a vote, the fusion picks the most-supported answer.

## Absorbs Existing Issues

- forge-9dcs (MCP-free extraction)
- forge-x20b (Use existing skills data for matching)

## Dependencies

- DEPENDS ON: Epic 1 (Skill Graph) — needs graph to query
- BLOCKS: Epic 3 (Alignment), Epic 5 (Browser Runtime)
- CROSS-CUTS: AI Engineering > NLP & Extraction (forge-ajxn) — RRF framework is reusable
- CROSS-CUTS: AI Engineering > Embeddings & Similarity (forge-4f47) — embedding model integration reusable

## Worktree Isolation

Suggested worktree: .claude/worktrees/skill-extraction
- Touches: packages/core/src/services/skill-extraction/, packages/core/src/services/embedding/
- CAN parallelize individual extractors (1-4) once RRF interface defined
- Safe to parallelize with: UI work, extension, Epic 4 (after Epic 1 stable)
- NOT safe to parallelize with: Epic 1 while schema in flux

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] All 4 extractors implemented and individually testable
- [ ] RRF fusion produces ranked skill list from arbitrary text
- [ ] Entity-specific strategies for JDs, bullets, certs, and free text
- [ ] Extraction quality >= current LLM approach on 20 test JDs
- [ ] Works without network calls (all data from local graph snapshot)
- [ ] Industry/domain context influences extraction when available
- [ ] New skill candidates flagged for curation review
- [ ] Level/experience information extracted alongside skills
- [ ] Performance: < 500ms for a typical JD on CPU
- [ ] Capture the work done in this session as a Memory, including what landed, key decisions encoded, lessons, and critical-path state. Save under /Users/adam/.claude/projects/-Users-adam-code-proj-forge/memory/ and update MEMORY.md index.

title: Skill Graph Data Model & Storage
description:
Design and implement the typed skill graph data model, storage layer, and snapshot format. This is the foundation that all other skill intelligence work builds on.

## Context

Forge skills are currently flat entities with categories and basic junction tables (bullet_skills, certification_skills). This epic transforms skills into a proper graph with typed relationships, alias resolution, hierarchical structure, and a snapshot format for client-side distribution.

Skills are the JOIN TABLE of the entire Forge system — they connect JDs to bullets, bullets to certifications, JDs to resumes. Every major relationship passes through skills. This epic builds the backbone.

## Graph Architecture Context

The Skill Graph is one of several graphs in Forge's information architecture:

1. **Skill Graph** (this epic) — taxonomy, similarity, co-occurrence between skills + temporal weights
2. **Provenance Graph** (existing, implicit) — Source → Bullet → Perspective → Resume Entry
3. **Alignment Graph** (Epic 3, computed) — JD requirements ↔ resume skills ↔ bullets
4. **Org Graph** (existing, to extend) — orgs + industries + campuses + industry→domain edges
5. **Domain/Archetype Graph** (future epic) — archetype→skill clusters, career paths, domain overlap
6. **Career Target Graph** (future epic) — aggregate experience vs target archetype gap analysis
7. **Certification Graph** (future epic) — vendor hierarchies, progression paths, skill validation levels

The Skill Graph connects to all of them. Schema decisions here constrain everything downstream.

## What This Epic Delivers

1. **Graph schema** — nodes (skills with canonical names, aliases, categories, level descriptors) + typed edges
2. **Storage layer** — SQLite tables (skill_graph_nodes, skill_graph_edges), HelixDB-compatible
3. **Embedding storage** — per-skill vectors with model version tracking
4. **Temporal edge metadata** — co-occurrence weights with time windows (enables job market trend analysis)
5. **Snapshot format** — serializable format for client distribution (nodes + edges + embeddings + HNSW index)
6. **Migration** — existing skills migrated into new schema

## Graph Schema Design

Nodes:
- id, canonical_name, category, description
- aliases: string[] (K8s, Kubernetes, kube)
- level_descriptors: JSON (how to interpret years/expertise levels for this skill)
- embedding: Float32Array (384-dim for MiniLM, with model_version FK)
- confidence: float (how well-established in the corpus)
- source: enum (seed, extracted, curated, user-created)

Edges (typed):
- source_id, target_id, edge_type, weight, confidence
- Edge types: alias-of, parent-of, child-of, prerequisite, related-to, co-occurs, platform-for
- Temporal metadata on co-occurs edges: {window_start, window_end, weight} array

## Seam: Skill Graph ↔ Extraction Pipeline (Epic 2)

Interface: the extraction pipeline queries the graph for candidate matching.
- Graph provides: HNSW index over embeddings, alias lookup table, adjacency list for traversal
- Pipeline returns: new skill candidates (HNSW misses), co-occurrence observations, confidence updates
- Contract: graph schema must be stable before extraction pipeline development begins

## Seam: Skill Graph ↔ Alignment Scoring (Epic 3)

Interface: alignment algorithm traverses the graph to compute match types.
- Edge types have numeric semantics: alias=1.0, child=0.9, parent=0.5, sibling=0.4, co-occurs=0.3
- Graph provides: typed traversal API (find_aliases, find_children, find_related, n_hop_neighbors)
- The graph does NOT compute alignment — it provides the traversal substrate

## Seam: Embedding Space ↔ Graph Space

Embeddings give 'nearby in meaning'. Graph gives 'nearby in structure'. Sometimes these agree (K8s and Docker close in both), sometimes they diverge (Python and JavaScript close in embeddings but no graph edge). The RRF fusion in Epic 2 is WHERE this seam gets resolved.

## Seam: Persistent Graph ↔ Computed Alignment Graph

The skill graph is persistent (stored, curated, evolving). The alignment graph (Epic 3) is ephemeral (computed per resume*JD pair). But alignment results can feed BACK into the persistent graph (co-occurrence statistics, new skill candidates).

## Snapshot Format

Target: ~20MB at 10k skills (384-dim embeddings)
- metadata: { version, skill_count, edge_count, embedding_model, embedding_dim }
- nodes: packed array (id, name, aliases, category)
- edges: adjacency list (typed, with temporal metadata)
- embeddings: Float32Array (parallel to nodes)
- hnsw_index: pre-built binary blob

Storage targets: Cloudflare R2 for snapshots, D1 for server-side graph, IndexedDB for browser cache.

## Seam: Server Graph ↔ Browser Snapshot

Server has full mutable graph. Browser has read-only snapshot. Writes flow: browser → server → graph update → new snapshot → browser. Version checking via ETag prevents unnecessary re-downloads.

## Absorbs Existing Issues

- forge-6bht (Skills dedup, aliasing, taxonomy/ontology)
- forge-zb9u (Skill relations — alias, parent, child, sibling, alternative)
- forge-7v6q (Skill alternative spellings / match patterns)

## Dependencies

- BLOCKS: Epic 2 (Extraction), Epic 3 (Alignment), Epic 4 (Curation), Epic 5 (Browser Runtime)
- Future: Domain/Archetype Graph, Career Target Graph, Cert Graph all build on this schema

## Worktree Isolation

Suggested worktree: .claude/worktrees/skill-graph-model
- Touches: packages/core/src/repositories/, packages/core/src/types/, packages/core/migrations/
- Safe to parallelize with: UI work, extension work, Rust rewrite
- NOT safe to parallelize with: Epic 2 or any skill extraction/matching code
- Migration work should be serialized

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] skill_graph_nodes and skill_graph_edges tables created with typed edge support
- [ ] Temporal metadata on co-occurrence edges
- [ ] Existing ~200 skills migrated with no data loss
- [ ] At least parent-of and alias-of edges populated
- [ ] Snapshot serialization/deserialization roundtrip tested
- [ ] Embedding storage with model version tracking
- [ ] Graph traversal queries working (children, aliases, N-hop neighbors)
- [ ] Snapshot size under 25MB for 10k simulated nodes
- [ ] Typed traversal API documented (contract for Epic 2 and 3)

title: Generated Resume 'Org title line' includes unexpected data in DnD Editor & LaTeX Preview
description:
The organization title line in generated resumes shows incorrect or unexpected data instead of the expected format.

**Expected**: Organization name only, or organization name + location
**Actual**: Location is being replaced with other metadata (e.g., contract status)

### Examples

| Expected | Actual |
|----------|--------|
| Raytheon Intelligence & Space (Arlington, VA) | Raytheon Intelligence & Space |
| Cisco (Remote) | Cisco (Contract) |
| Greymatter.io (Remote) | Greymatter.io (Contract) |

### Observations
- Location data is either missing entirely (Raytheon case) or replaced with employment type/contract status (Cisco, Greymatter cases)
- Affects both the DnD Editor view and the LaTeX Preview
- Suggests the org title line formatter is pulling from the wrong field — likely `source_type` or a role relationship field instead of `modality`/location

### Where to look
- Resume IR compiler: `crates/forge-sdk/src/services/compiler_service.rs` (Rust) / `packages/core/src/services/compiler-service.ts` (TS)
- Resume entry rendering logic for experience sections
- How org location / modality is resolved for the title line
notes:
## Root Cause Investigation (2026-04-24)

### Finding: TS compiler is CORRECT
Ran `buildOrgDisplayString` directly against live data:
- Cisco → `Cisco (Remote)` ✓ (uses work_arrangement, city/state null)
- Raytheon → `Raytheon Intelligence & Space (Arlington, VA)` ✓ (uses city/state)
- Greymatter.io → `Greymatter.io (Remote)` ✓ (uses work_arrangement)

The TS code at `packages/core/src/services/resume-compiler.ts:1093` correctly prioritizes
city/state over work_arrangement. WebUI calls TS API server.

### Finding: Rust compiler IS BROKEN
`crates/forge-sdk/src/services/compiler_service.rs:690`:
```rust
let org_display = first.org_name.clone().unwrap_or_else(|| org_key.clone());
```
This is BARE ORG NAME — no location, no work_arrangement parenthetical.
Missing the equivalent of TS `buildOrgDisplayString(org_name, city, state, work_arrangement)`.

### Finding: Misleading doc comment (TS)
Line 1082 says `Format: {org_name}{ (employment_type)}` but code uses `work_arrangement`.
Doc comment is stale from a prior design — may have confused the reporter.

### Fix
1. Rust: add `build_org_display_string()` to compiler_service.rs, use at line 690
2. TS: fix stale doc comment at line 1082
close_reason: Root cause: Rust compiler used bare org name at line 690 instead of calling buildOrgDisplayString equivalent. Added build_org_display_string() with location/arrangement priority logic + 7 tests. TS code was correct but had stale doc comment (fixed). 360 tests passing.

title: Skills deduplication, aliasing, and taxonomy/ontology design
description:
Review how skills are identified and deduplicated during extraction. Some skills are aliases/variants of another (e.g. 'K8s' vs 'Kubernetes'). Need to define: (1) How to construct the taxonomy/ontology of skills, (2) What qualifies as an alias/variant vs a distinct skill, (3) How to identify and normalize requested skill levels (e.g. '5 years' vs 'expert' vs 'knowledgeable' vs 'familiar'). title: forge_approve_perspective must require human-in-the-loop review
description:
forge_approve_perspective currently allows an AI agent to approve perspectives without human verification. This is dangerous — perspectives are derived by AI from bullets, and approving them without human review means hallucinated or inaccurate content can flow directly into resumes.

## Problem
An MCP client (Claude) can call forge_prepare_derivation → forge_commit_derivation → forge_approve_perspective in a single automated flow. The approve step has no gate requiring the human to actually read and verify the perspective content before it becomes resume-ready.

## Risk
- AI-generated perspectives may contain hallucinated accomplishments
- Subtle inaccuracies (wrong metrics, inflated scope) pass unchecked
- Approved perspectives are added to resumes and exported to PDF/LaTeX
- User's professional reputation depends on accuracy of these statements

## Proposed fix
forge_approve_perspective should:
1. Return a confirmation prompt that includes the full perspective content
2. Require the human to explicitly confirm after seeing the content
3. Consider adding a "reviewed_by" field (human vs agent) to track provenance
4. Optionally: require perspectives to sit in "pending_review" for a minimum time before approval is allowed

## Workaround
Current mitigation: use forge_review_pending to list all pending perspectives, manually review each, then approve individually. But nothing enforces this workflow.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Capture the work done in this session as a Memory, including what landed, key decisions encoded, lessons, and critical-path state. Save under /Users/adam/.claude/projects/-Users-adam-code-proj-forge/memory/ and update MEMORY.md index.

title: Retrofit tests: acceptance, component, and contract coverage for already-landed phases
description:
Option C retrofit from the Qualifications planning session (2026-04-05). The
Qualifications track going forward is following Option A (explicit
acceptance tests + component tests + contract tests per task). This issue
captures the equivalent retrofit work for phases that have already landed:

Missing acceptance / component / contract tests on main:

1. **Phase 89 (Skills Expansion)** — merged 2026-04-05
   - No explicit acceptance tests mapping to T89.1-T89.7 plan criteria.
   - Existing coverage is implementation tests (skill repo, junction CRUD,
     migration-031). Gap: assert each "Acceptance Criteria" bullet from
     the plan maps to a test case.

2. **Phase 91 (Summaries Rework)** — merged 2026-04-05
   - No structural component test for `SummaryDetailModal.svelte` (modal
     state, skill picker, industry/role_type dropdowns).
   - No structural component test for the rewritten
     `/resumes/summaries/+page.svelte` (sticky headers, group-by,
     sort-by, clickable rows).
   - No acceptance tests for T91.1-T91.6 criteria.

3. **Phase 92 (Tagline Engine)** — merged 2026-04-05
   - No structural component test for `HeaderEditor.svelte` rewrite
     (override vs generated display, regenerate button, reset).
   - Tagline service has strong unit tests (27) — fine.
   - Integration tests for JD link/unlink regeneration exist — fine.
   - Gap: contract tests for the new `/resumes/:id/tagline` endpoints.

4. **Phase 94 (Resume Source Tab Rendering)** — merged earlier
   - No component tests for the rendered markdown / LaTeX preview paths.

Pattern to follow: the project's convention is **structural** component
tests (read source file + assert on patterns with regex/string-match),
not runtime rendering. See:
- packages/webui/src/__tests__/jd-overlay.test.ts
- packages/webui/src/__tests__/component-adoption.test.ts
- packages/webui/src/__tests__/interactive-systems.test.ts

For contract tests, follow the existing pattern in
packages/core/src/routes/__tests__/contracts.test.ts.

Blocks: nothing urgent — this is maintenance. Pick up after the
Qualifications track ships and before the next feature rollout.

title: MCP forge://resume/{id} resource returns 'not found' for all resume IDs
description: The MCP resource handler for forge://resume/{id} returns 'Resource ... not found' (-32602) for every resume ID tested (Thomson Reuters 1cb1f654, Woven df5942ea, Pelagic AI fbd6a97a, Chainguard 93d1bb8a — 5 distinct IDs across statuses approved/in_review/draft). The REST endpoint GET /api/resumes/:id at port 3000 returns the data correctly. The /ir variant (forge://resume/{id}/ir) has the same issue. Discovered 2026-05-05 during a resume-building session — agents using forge-mcp must fall back to direct REST calls. Resource templates (forge://templates), forge://archetypes, and forge://profile work fine, so the issue is specific to the resume URI template handler. Likely a missing handler registration or URI template parsing bug in the MCP resource layer.

title: Re-evaluate forge/resumx engineering-vs-consumer namespace split
description:
The forge/resumx engineering-vs-consumer namespace split (per .claude/rules/workers.md) bought protection against hypothetical futures (forge product pivot, resumx spin-off, third product) at real present-day costs. Friction surfaced concretely during forge-dfwx (TF Phase 0) when the spec-prescribed `tf-prod-<component>-<verb>` 1P Environment naming clashed with the existing 1P convention (`<ext>-<scope>-<deployment>`, e.g., `gh-resumx-production`).

## Pressure points observed

1. **1P-naming friction.** External-consumer-anchored 1P conventions don't naturally accommodate engineering/consumer abstraction layers.
2. **The boundary is fuzzy in practice.** `tf-apply-cf.yml` is engineering (CI/IaC) and consumer (deploys to `resu.mx` infra) simultaneously; the split forces a call where the underlying object is genuinely both.
3. **MVP single-user phase doesn't pay back the abstraction yet.** It buys against hypotheticals; none imminent.

## Currently coupled to the split

- `workers/auth/wrangler.jsonc` — Worker name `resumx-auth`, env vars `RESUMX_*`, custom domain `auth.resu.mx` (live)
- `sites/landing/wrangler.jsonc` — CF Pages name `resumx-landing`, custom domain `resu.mx` (live)
- `.claude/rules/workers.md` — entire "Worker name & hostname" + "Env naming" sections
- `.claude/rules/branding.md` — references TBD `resumx` brand source-of-truth
- `.claude/rules/beads-taxonomy.md` (forge-infra) — uses `forge` for repo categories
- `forge-tf-state` R2 bucket name (spec'd, not yet imported)
- 1P vault `forge-infra-tf` (live)

## Decision dimensions

1. **Keep** the split (status quo). Friction survives; abstraction matures.
2. **Collapse to forge-***: rename `resumx-auth`→`forge-auth`, `RESUMX_*`→`FORGE_*`, etc. Touches 5+ live artifacts.
3. **Collapse to resumx-***: rename `forge-tf-state`→`resumx-tf-state`, beads project, repo branding. Touches TF substrate + repo identity.
4. **Partial**: collapse only in *new* artifacts; let in-flight artifacts age out.

## Trigger / forcing function

forge-dfwx Phase 0 1P Environment naming. Workaround applied: 1P Env names follow the existing `gh-<repo>-<deployment>` convention (`gh-forge-infra-tf-cf-plan` etc.), without resolving the broader question. Decision should land before the next `resumx-*` or `RESUMX_*` artifact lands (forge-xk0z.23 DNS imports may surface more friction).

## Out of scope for this bead

- Implementation of whichever choice (separate landing).
- Brand source-of-truth (`branding.md` TBD); pre-decides consumer brand identity if collapse-to-forge wins.

## Decision rule (until decided)

Block: any new artifact that would otherwise commit to one side or the other beyond what's already live. Document workarounds applied in affected spec revision histories.
acceptance_criteria: (1) Decision recorded as ADR under `docs/src/adrs/` per docs-adrs.md migration rules. (2) All in-flight workarounds (Phase 0 1P Env naming, etc.) revisited under the chosen direction. (3) Affected artifacts (workers.md, branding.md, beads-taxonomy.md, this repo's CLAUDE.md) updated to reflect the decision. (4) If collapse path is chosen: migration plan filed as a separate beads epic.

title: Terraform Phase 1.1–1.3 — Laptop bootstrap (skeleton HCL, tofu init, R2 state-bucket import)
description:
Implements Phase 1.1–1.3 (laptop-only bootstrap) per .claude/plans/forge-terraform-bootstrap/refs/specs/2026-04-28-tf-state-design.md §6.

## What lands

- **`terraform/cf/`** module with:
  - `backend.tf` — R2 S3-compat backend, `use_lockfile = true`, key `prod/cf/terraform.tfstate`, endpoint hardcoded to `https://90f8f70e393d3a299e83b8cf39161715.r2.cloudflarestorage.com`.
  - `versions.tf` — `required_version = ">= 1.10"` and `cloudflare/cloudflare` provider pinned (use `tofu providers schema -json` to confirm latest stable major; spec §1 references v5).
  - `encryption.tf` — PBKDF2 + AES-GCM, `enforced = true`, passphrase from `var.tf_state_passphrase`.
  - `providers.tf` — `provider "cloudflare" {}`; CF account ID `90f8f70e393d3a299e83b8cf39161715` declared as a `local` for resource references.
  - `variables.tf` — `tf_state_passphrase` (sensitive). NO `cf_account_id` variable (spec §1: hardcoded).
  - `main.tf` — empty for 1.1, then state-bucket resource added in 1.3.
- **`.opentofu-version`** at repo root with the exact pin (e.g., `1.11.0`).
- **`.gitignore`** at repo root — add `.envrc` (per spec §4 — direnv-based `op://...` resolution; gitignored).

## Procedure (laptop only — uses `tf-bootstrap-operator-cf` token)

1. **1.1** Author skeleton config above. Run `tofu validate` — must pass.
2. **1.2** `cd terraform/cf && op run -- tofu init`. Expect: R2 reachable, encryption passphrase resolves, lock file writable, no resources yet. If 1.2 fails, fail before introducing import complexity.
3. **1.3** Add to `main.tf`:

```hcl
# engineering namespace per .claude/rules/workers.md asymmetry —
# do not "fix" to resumx-tf-state; this is operator-facing, not consumer-facing.
resource "cloudflare_r2_bucket" "tf_state" {
  account_id = local.cf_account_id
  name       = "forge-tf-state"
  location   = "WNAM"  # match what was used at create time in Phase 0.4
  lifecycle {
    prevent_destroy = true
  }
}
```

   Run:
   - `op run -- tofu import cloudflare_r2_bucket.tf_state 90f8f70e393d3a299e83b8cf39161715/forge-tf-state`
   - `op run -- tofu plan` — must show **zero diff**. Adjust `location` to match reality if needed; do NOT remove `prevent_destroy`.
   - `op run -- tofu apply` — operator confirms; expected to be a no-op.

## Verification

- `op run -- tofu state list` shows `cloudflare_r2_bucket.tf_state`.
- `op run -- tofu show cloudflare_r2_bucket.tf_state` shows `name = "forge-tf-state"` and a non-empty `location`.
- A second `op run -- tofu plan` is clean (zero diff, idempotent).

## Anti-patterns

- Do NOT add a `var.cf_account_id` variable — spec §1 hardcodes it.
- Do NOT commit `.envrc` — must be in root `.gitignore`.
- Do NOT remove `prevent_destroy` on the state bucket.
- Do NOT touch `terraform/github/` here — that root module lands with the first GH-side import.
- Do NOT pin `aRustyDev/load-secrets-action@main` anywhere; if a workflow lands alongside this, see forge-cw83.
- Do NOT author CI workflows in this bead — Phase 2 belongs to forge-xk0z.24.

## Depends on

- forge-dfwx (Phase 0 manual prereqs).
- Parent design: forge-xk0z.21.

## Out of scope

- DNS, Pages, D1, KV, Worker custom_domain imports (Phase 1.4a–d) — forge-xk0z.23 / .25 / .26 / .27.
- CI workflow authoring (Phase 2) — forge-xk0z.24.
- `terraform/github/` root module — lands with first GH-side import (Phase 3 / forge-xk0z.28).

title: Terraform Phase 0 — Manual prerequisites (1Password, CF tokens, R2 bucket, GH Environments)
description:
Implements Phase 0 (manual prerequisites) per .claude/plans/forge-terraform-bootstrap/refs/specs/2026-04-28-tf-state-design.md §6.

## What lands (operator-side; no code)

Substrate prerequisites that must exist before any TF code can run. Spec steps 0.1–0.10:

- **0.1** Generate two state passphrases (`openssl rand -base64 64`); store in 1Password vault `forge-infra-tf` as `state-key-cf` and `state-key-github` (field: `passphrase`).
- **0.2** CF API token `tf-bootstrap-operator-cf` with full Edit scope (Workers/KV/D1/Pages/R2 Account, DNS/Zone/Page Rules/Transform Rules Zone resu.mx); store in operator's personal 1Password vault.
- **0.3** GH PAT `tf-bootstrap-operator-gh` (`repo`, `workflow`, `admin:repo_hook`); store in operator's personal 1Password vault.
- **0.4** `wrangler r2 bucket create forge-tf-state`; enable Object Versioning via dashboard (CLI path may exist by impl time — re-verify).
- **0.5** Generate R2 access key + secret scoped to `forge-tf-state` only (Object Read & Write); store in 1Password vault `forge-infra-tf` as `r2-state-credentials` (`access_key_id`, `secret_access_key`).
- **0.6** Create four 1Password Environments under vault `forge-infra-tf`: `tf-prod-cf-plan`, `tf-prod-cf-apply`, `tf-prod-gh-plan`, `tf-prod-gh-apply`.
- **0.7** Generate four narrow CF/GH tokens (`tf-plan-cf`, `tf-apply-cf`, `tf-plan-gh`, `tf-apply-gh`) per spec §6 Phase 0.7 table; place in matching 1Password Environments along with passphrase + R2 cred refs.
- **0.8** Create 1Password Service Account scoped read-only to vault `forge-infra-tf` + 4 Environments; generate SA token.
- **0.9** Create four GH Environments matching the 1Password names; add Required Reviewer (operator) on `*-apply`.
- **0.10** Add to each GH Environment two secrets only: `OP_SVC_ACCT_TOKEN` (the SA token from 0.8) and `OP_ENVIRONMENT_ID` (the corresponding 1Password Environment ID).

## Acceptance

- All four 1Password Environments populated and resolvable via `op` CLI.
- All four GH Environments exist with the two secrets configured.
- R2 bucket `forge-tf-state` exists with Object Versioning ON.
- R2 creds validate via smoke test (e.g., `op run -- aws s3 ls --endpoint https://<account_id>.r2.cloudflarestorage.com` resolves successfully).
- Inventory captured in bead notes: 1P Environment IDs, GH Environment names, R2 bucket location. NEVER commit secret values.

## Anti-patterns

- Do NOT commit any secret value.
- Do NOT name the 1Password Environments `forge-*` — naming-inversion per spec §3 means Environments use `tf-prod-<component>-<verb>`.
- Do NOT skip R2 Object Versioning — the `prevent_destroy` safety net (Phase 1.3) depends on it for catastrophic-error recovery.
- Do NOT use `@main` pinning anywhere; this bead is operator-side only, but the principle applies to forge-cw83.

## References

- Spec: `.claude/plans/forge-terraform-bootstrap/refs/specs/2026-04-28-tf-state-design.md` §6 Phase 0.
- Parent design: forge-xk0z.21.
- Companion: forge-cw83 (`aRustyDev/load-secrets-action` v1 release / SHA pin policy).
notes:
2026-04-29 — Phase 0 walkthrough in-progress.

## Step 0.1 complete (with naming deviations)

State-encryption passphrases generated and stored.

## As-built 1P inventory (deviates from spec design — capture for spec amendment)

### Items (no underlying vault; items held inside 1P Envs directly)
- 'state_key_cf' (field 'passphrase') — held in Env 'gh-forge-infra-tf-cf'
- 'state_key_github' (field 'passphrase') — held in Env 'gh-forge-infra-tf-gh'

(Item titles use underscore not hyphen — '_' permitted, '-' was not.)

### 1P Environments (6 total — 2 shared + 4 narrow)
- gh-forge-infra-tf-cf       (shared cf-state: state_key_cf passphrase + R2 creds [pending step 0.5])
- gh-forge-infra-tf-cf-plan  (narrow: will hold tf-plan-cf token  [step 0.7])
- gh-forge-infra-tf-cf-apply (narrow: will hold tf-apply-cf token [step 0.7])
- gh-forge-infra-tf-gh       (shared gh-state: state_key_github passphrase + R2 creds)
- gh-forge-infra-tf-gh-plan  (narrow: will hold tf-plan-gh PAT  [step 0.7])
- gh-forge-infra-tf-gh-apply (narrow: will hold tf-apply-gh PAT [step 0.7])

### 1P SA tokens (4 total — per state × per verb) and their Env-Read access
- gh-forge-infra-tf-cf-read  → reads [gh-forge-infra-tf-cf, gh-forge-infra-tf-cf-plan]
- gh-forge-infra-tf-cf-write → reads [gh-forge-infra-tf-cf, gh-forge-infra-tf-cf-apply]
- gh-forge-infra-tf-gh-read  → reads [gh-forge-infra-tf-gh, gh-forge-infra-tf-gh-plan]
- gh-forge-infra-tf-gh-write → reads [gh-forge-infra-tf-gh, gh-forge-infra-tf-gh-apply]

### Workflow secret-injection model
Each GH Environment in 'aRustyDev/forge-infra' carries:
- OP_SVC_ACCT_TOKEN (the matching 1P SA token from the four above)
- OP_ENVIRONMENT_ID_SHARED (the matching shared Env ID)
- OP_ENVIRONMENT_ID_NARROW (the matching narrow Env ID)
Workflows call 'aRustyDev/load-secrets-action' twice (shared + narrow) per job.
GH Environment names: tf-cf-plan, tf-cf-apply, tf-gh-plan, tf-gh-apply.

## Deviations from spec (require amendment at session-end)
- Item naming: spec uses hyphens ('state-key-cf'); 1P UI required underscores → 'state_key_cf'.
- No vault: items in Envs directly. Spec §4 wording 'scoped to vault X and the Envs' needs revision.
- Env naming convention: spec used 'tf-prod-<component>-<verb>'; replaced with 'gh-forge-infra-tf-<state>[-<verb>]' to match operator's existing 1P pattern ('<ext>-<repo>-<deployment>'; cf existing 'gh-resumx-production').
- Env count: 4 → 6 (2 shared + 4 narrow). Preserves §3 plan/apply IAM split without passphrase-ref duplication.
- SA count: 1 → 4 (per state × verb). Stronger isolation, x4 rotation cost.
- Workflow injection: 2 load-secrets-action calls per job, 3 GH-Env-secrets per GH Env (vs spec's 1 call, 2 secrets).

## Spec amendments needed (session-end task)
- §3 IAM table: rewrite for 6-Env + 4-SA model.
- §4 Secret injection: 2 load-secrets calls; GH Env holds 3 secrets.
- §6 Phase 0.6: 'create 4 Envs under vault' → 'create 6 Envs (no underlying vault)'.
- §6 Phase 0.7: token-mapping table updated for 6-Env layout.
- §6 Phase 0.10: each GH Env holds 3 secrets (1 SA token + 2 Env IDs).
- §7 State-encryption rotation SOP: tweak for Env-stored (not vault-stored) passphrases.

## Open items (not blocking Phase 0)
- Laptop path (forge-f6i1): 'op' CLI resolution syntax for Env-stored items. Spec §4 assumes 'op://vault/item/field'. Resolve before forge-f6i1.
- forge-c9ds filed: forge/resumx namespace split decision. Not blocking dfwx; workaround captured in this design.

## Steps remaining
0.2 — CF API token tf-bootstrap-operator-cf (operator's personal 1P, NOT in TF Envs).
0.3 — GH PAT tf-bootstrap-operator-gh (operator's personal 1P).
0.4 — wrangler r2 bucket create forge-tf-state + Object Versioning ON (dashboard).
0.5 — R2 access keys (Object Read & Write, scoped to forge-tf-state); store and ref into shared Envs.
0.6 — Already done (the 6 Envs above).
0.7 — Generate 4 narrow CF/GH tokens; place in narrow Envs.
0.8 — Already partially done; reframe as 'create 4 SA tokens with Env scoping'.
0.9 — Create 4 GH Environments matching tf-cf-plan / tf-cf-apply / tf-gh-plan / tf-gh-apply; Required Reviewer on *-apply.
0.10 — Add 3 secrets to each GH Env (SA token + shared Env ID + narrow Env ID).

title: Terraform Phase 3 — GitHub side (rulesets + repo + environments + vars + CODEOWNERS)
description:
Implements Phase 3 per .claude/plans/forge-terraform-bootstrap/refs/specs/2026-04-29-terraform-contents-design.md §4 + §5 + §8.

## What lands

- terraform/github/repository.tf with github_repository.forge_infra (settings per spec §5: name, description, default_branch, allow_squash_merge=true, allow_merge_commit=false, allow_rebase_merge=false, delete_branch_on_merge=true, has_issues=false, vulnerability_alerts=true)
- terraform/github/environments.tf with github_repository_environment × 4 (tf-prod-{cf,gh}-{plan,apply}); apply Environments require 1 reviewer
- terraform/github/variables.tf with github_actions_environment_variable × 4 for OP_ENVIRONMENT_ID (per Environment)
- terraform/github/rulesets.tf with three rulesets: branch (~DEFAULT_BRANCH conditions), push (file-path/extension/size), tag (~ALL, signed + non-deletion)
- .github/CODEOWNERS (a code file, not TF) — covers .github/workflows/, terraform/, .claude/rules/

## Migration step (REQUIRED before var-create apply)

The OP_ENVIRONMENT_ID name was provisioned as a SECRET in xk0z.21 Phase 0.10. GH does not allow same-name secret + var to coexist. Procedure (spec §8 step 3):

1. For each of 4 Environments, delete OP_ENVIRONMENT_ID secret via dashboard
2. tofu apply to create OP_ENVIRONMENT_ID var with same value (UUID is not secret)
3. Verify workflows can load via vars.OP_ENVIRONMENT_ID (NOT secrets.)

Workflows authored in Phase 2 already reference vars.OP_ENVIRONMENT_ID per spec §7's example.

## Branch ruleset deferred-deployment rule

Per spec §5 (chicken-and-egg avoidance): do NOT include "Required deployments" in the initial branch-ruleset apply. Add it in a follow-up small PR after Phase 2's no-op validation has shipped through both apply Environments (so deployment history exists).

## Verify before authoring HCL (spec Open Questions)

- GitHub provider ruleset target syntax: conditions { ref_name { include = ["~DEFAULT_BRANCH"] } } vs flatter shape; verify against integrations/github provider docs.
- github_repository_ruleset attribute shape across provider versions.

## Bypass-actor list

Both rulesets must include aRustyDev's GitHub account in the bypass-actor list (spec Risks: lockout-recovery). The 1Password Service Account's bot identity (if it has one) goes here too.

## Acceptance

- All listed resources under TF state
- tofu plan zero diff
- Branch ruleset enforces against a no-op test PR
- Push ruleset blocks a deliberately-bad path push (test fixture, then revert)
- CODEOWNERS triggers required-review on workflows/terraform changes
- github_repository import shows zero diff (settings drift fixed in HCL before apply)

## Depends on

- Phase 2 (CI cutover).

title: Terraform Phase 1.4c — Import resumx-auth-db (D1) + resumx-auth-kv (KV)
description:
Implements Phase 1.4c per .claude/plans/forge-terraform-bootstrap/refs/specs/2026-04-29-terraform-contents-design.md §6.

## What lands

- terraform/cf/d1.tf with cloudflare_d1_database.auth + lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true }
- terraform/cf/kv.tf with cloudflare_workers_kv_namespace.auth + lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true }

## prevent_destroy is REQUIRED

D1 and KV resource IDs are referenced from workers/auth/wrangler.jsonc as hardcoded strings:
- database_id (line 15): 061c98e5-0bda-4164-8b12-d1fb270da305
- kv id (line 21): 5817fe6572e24f109ef71f671c3a55f5

A destroy-and-recreate would silently break the runtime bindings (spec §10 four-field cross-tool contract).

## Resource-only (no bindings)

Per spec §1 (hard split = B, Q1): TF creates the D1/KV by name. wrangler.jsonc owns the binding (D1 → RESUMX_AUTH_DB, KV → RESUMX_AUTH_KV). This bead does NOT touch wrangler.jsonc.

## Per-resource dry-run pattern (spec §6).

## Acceptance

- D1 and KV resources under TF state
- tofu plan zero diff
- prevent_destroy on both
- wrangler.jsonc unchanged
- wrangler dev still works (sanity check: cd workers/auth && just dev → http://localhost:8787 responds)

## Depends on

- Phase 2 (CI cutover).

title: Terraform Phase 1.4d — Import Worker custom_domain (rolling-cut on wrangler.jsonc)
description:
Implements Phase 1.4d per .claude/plans/forge-terraform-bootstrap/refs/specs/2026-04-29-terraform-contents-design.md §6 + §1 + §10.

## What lands

- terraform/cf/workers.tf with cloudflare_workers_custom_domain.auth (binds auth.resu.mx → resumx-auth)
- workers/auth/wrangler.jsonc routes block removed (Phase 6b — gated on 6a verification, see callout below)
- .claude/rules/workers.md updated to reflect outcome (single-claim or dual-claim variant)

## CRITICAL — Read the spec §6 callout before starting

Steps 1-5 (import) are the core work. Step 6 (cleanup) is OPTIONAL and verification-gated:

- Step 6a: deploy with routes block removed in low-traffic window; verify auth.resu.mx still resolves. Revert immediately if it doesn't (TF still owns the custom_domain in state; the next tofu apply will re-attach it).
- Step 6b: only after 6a passes, land routes-block-removal as a permanent commit.

If 6a fails (wrangler version auto-detaches the live route): dual-claim is the documented final state. workers.md gets the dual-claim variant. No outage; the import work is still done.

## Verify before authoring HCL (spec Open Questions)

- Custom_domain attribute name: tofu providers schema -json | jq for the resource schema — adjust HCL between .script vs .service + .environment per v5 schema.
- Import ID format: tofu import -help cloudflare_workers_custom_domain — adjust import command between <id> alone vs <account_id>/<id>.

## NOT imported

- cloudflare_workers_script (per spec §1 — TF doesn't manage the Worker entity itself; Workers Builds owns it).

## Acceptance

- Worker custom_domain under TF state
- auth.resu.mx still resolves end-to-end (curl check before AND after step 6)
- workers.md updated to reflect 6a outcome
- One of: routes block removed (6a/6b path) OR routes block retained with dual-claim documented (6a-failed path)

## Depends on

- Phase 2 (CI cutover) — applies via CI
- Phase 1.4a (DNS) — auth.resu.mx record must be in TF state first

## Unblocks

- forge-xk0z.8 (rate-limit WAF) — needs the route in TF state
- forge-xk0z.14 (CORS Transform Rules) — needs the route in TF state

title: Terraform Phase 2 — CI cutover (workflows for tf-{plan,apply}-{cf,gh})
description:
Implements Phase 2 per .claude/plans/forge-terraform-bootstrap/refs/specs/2026-04-29-terraform-contents-design.md §7.

## What lands

- .github/workflows/tf-plan-cf.yml
- .github/workflows/tf-apply-cf.yml
- .github/workflows/tf-plan-gh.yml
- .github/workflows/tf-apply-gh.yml

Each loads its 1Password Environment via aRustyDev/load-secrets-action (SHA-pinned per xk0z.21 §4). Path-filtered (terraform/cf/** or terraform/github/**). Concurrency control on apply workflows. Apply workflows declare environment: tf-prod-{cf,gh}-apply for the GH-Env reviewer gate.

## Validation

- Open a no-op PR through terraform/cf/ → plan workflow runs, apply gates on reviewer, apply succeeds with zero diff
- Repeat for terraform/github/

## Cutover

After validation, all applies go through CI; laptop apply is reserved for break-glass per xk0z.21 §5. Document the cutover date in the operator runbook.

## Acceptance

- Four workflows committed under .github/workflows/
- Both plan-and-apply paths validated end-to-end with no-op PRs
- aRustyDev/load-secrets-action pinned to a SHA or tagged release (NOT @main) per xk0z.21 §4 / Risks

## Depends on

- Phase 1.4a (small but real state to validate against; stronger than empty-state validation per spec §7).

title: Terraform Phase 1.4b — Import resumx-landing Pages project
description:
Implements Phase 1.4b per .claude/plans/forge-terraform-bootstrap/refs/specs/2026-04-29-terraform-contents-design.md §6.

## What lands

- terraform/cf/pages.tf with cloudflare_pages_project.landing
- lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true } on the resource (spec Risks: git-connection auth is account-scoped and not reproducible from HCL alone)

## Pre-import inspection (REQUIRED)

Dump live Pages-project config to a sidecar file before authoring HCL:

  wrangler pages project list --json > /tmp/pages-landing-snapshot.json

Then field-by-field author HCL to match. Build_config and deployment_configs blocks must mirror dashboard values exactly for clean import.

## Per-resource dry-run pattern (spec §6).

## Caveats

- Pages-project git-connection auth is account-scoped, not reproducible from HCL alone (spec Risks). prevent_destroy is the safety net.
- State recovery requires manual reconnection via Cloudflare dashboard → Pages → resumx-landing → Settings → Builds & deployments.

## Acceptance

- Pages project under TF state
- tofu plan shows zero diff
- prevent_destroy verified
- DNS record for the Pages domain (imported in 1.4a) gets a depends_on for clarity

## Depends on

- Phase 2 (CI cutover): apply runs through CI from this point.

title: Terraform Phase 1.4a — Import DNS records for resu.mx zone
description:
Implements Phase 1.4a per .claude/plans/forge-terraform-bootstrap/refs/specs/2026-04-29-terraform-contents-design.md §6.

## What lands

- terraform/cf/dns.tf with cloudflare_dns_record resources for every existing record in the resu.mx zone
- Per-resource dry-run pattern (Phase 1.4z, spec §6): write HCL → tofu plan (expect create) → tofu import → tofu plan (expect zero diff) → tofu apply (no-op)

## Sub-decision (open per spec §6)

Records-only vs zone-also (recommendation: records-only). Confirm with operator before authoring HCL. If zone-level config (SSL mode, security defaults) drift becomes a concern later, file a follow-up bead to import the zone resource itself.

## Inventory step

Run wrangler dns records list (or dashboard inventory) to enumerate live records in resu.mx before authoring HCL. Output goes in the bead notes for traceability.

## Acceptance

- All live DNS records under TF state
- tofu plan shows zero diff
- One small bash script (uncommitted, in bead notes) for the per-record import loop

## Depends on

- xk0z.21 implementation (state bucket import, Phase 1.3) must land before this can begin.
notes:
2026-04-29: substrate gap surfaced.

xk0z.21 was approved at the spec layer on 2026-04-28 but no Phase 0 / Phase 1.1-1.3 implementation beads existed. Filed:

- forge-dfwx (Phase 0 manual prereqs)
