title: Skill Graph Browser Runtime
description:
Skill graph operations running in the browser via forge-wasm (Rust WASM). This epic is now a MODULE within forge-wasm (R1.5), not a standalone JS implementation.

## Revised Context (browser-first + Rust WASM)

Originally designed as a JS browser runtime (transformers.js, usearch-js, plain JS graph). Now: skill graph operations are implemented in Rust and compiled to WASM as part of the forge-wasm crate.

## What This Module Contains (within forge-wasm)

- HNSW index: usearch-rs or hnswlib-rs compiled to WASM (not usearch-js)
- Graph runtime: petgraph loaded from snapshot (not plain JS Maps)
- Snapshot loader: deserialize global graph into petgraph + HNSW
- Skill search/autocomplete: embed query + HNSW top-k + fuzzy match
- Integration with extraction pipeline and alignment scoring (same crate)

## What Remains JS-Side

- Embedding model: transformers.js still runs in JS/WASM (separate from forge-wasm)
- The embedding model is called from Svelte, results passed to forge-wasm for HNSW lookup
- OR: ONNX runtime compiled into forge-wasm for fully Rust-side embedding (research needed)
- IndexedDB/OPFS cache management (browser APIs, accessed via web-sys)

## Snapshot Lifecycle (unchanged)

CDN hosts snapshot → browser downloads → IndexedDB cache → forge-wasm loads into petgraph + HNSW

## Performance Targets (unchanged)

- Snapshot load: < 500ms
- HNSW search: < 50ms at 10k
- Full extraction: < 500ms
- Alignment: < 100ms
- Memory: < 150MB total

## Dependencies

- DEPENDS ON: forge-ucc2 (Skill Graph Data Model — snapshot format)
- DEPENDS ON: forge-czgq (Extraction Pipeline — pipeline logic)
- PART OF: forge-6z5l (R1.5 forge-wasm crate)

See docs/src/architecture/models/runtime.md and docs/src/architecture/models/deployment.md

title: Skill Graph Curation & Growth
description:
Design and implement the workflow for seeding, growing, and maintaining quality of the skill graph. The graph is a living artifact that evolves from usage.

## Context

The skill graph starts at ~200 skills and needs to grow to 10k+. This requires: (1) bulk seeding from public taxonomies, (2) LLM-assisted relationship proposal, (3) human-in-the-loop review, and (4) automatic growth from extraction and alignment usage patterns.

The curation workflow also manages temporal metadata — tracking how skill demand changes over time (Job Market Epistemology), computed from co-occurrence statistics across ingested JDs.

## Seeding Strategy (3 layers)

### Layer 1: Public Taxonomies (bulk seed)
- ESCO (European Skills/Competences classification) — 13k+ skills, hierarchical, CC-BY
- O*NET (US Dept of Labor) — occupation-indexed skills, free
- Import: parse taxonomy → create nodes → create parent-of/child-of edges → compute embeddings

### Layer 2: Domain Extraction from Existing Data
- Run LLM extraction across all existing JDs and bullets in Forge
- Cluster results, propose canonical names and relationships
- Generate co-occurrence edges from statistical analysis
- Human reviews proposed merges and new nodes

### Layer 3: Ongoing Growth from Usage
- Confirmed extractions reinforce existing nodes (increment confidence)
- HNSW misses (new skill candidates) queue for review
- Co-occurrence statistics update edge weights continuously with temporal windowing
- Skills absent from recent JDs get flagged as potentially deprecated
- Periodic LLM pass proposes new edges between existing nodes

## Temporal Edge Metadata (Job Market Epistemology)

Co-occurrence edges accumulate time-windowed weights from JD processing:
- Each JD processed contributes to co-occurrence counts for its time window
- Windows: quarterly or monthly, depending on JD volume
- Enables: 'Terraform demand is declining in DevOps JDs while Pulumi is rising'
- Requires: 500+ JDs for meaningful signals, degrades gracefully below that

## Quality Control

- Human review gates: new skills, new edges, proposed merges
- Confidence thresholds: auto-accept > 0.95, review 0.7-0.95, auto-reject < 0.7
- Duplicate detection: embedding similarity + alias matching before creating new nodes
- forge-fi01 (Skills public/private flag): public skills are curated, user-created are private until promoted

## LLM Curation Workflow (build-time, not runtime)

- Batch: present skill pairs to LLM, ask 'What is the relationship?'
- LLM proposes edge type + confidence
- High-confidence auto-applied, others queued for human review
- Runs on server (can use full-size models)
- Results baked into next graph snapshot

## Seam: Curation ↔ Extraction Pipeline (Epic 2)

Extraction events are the primary growth signal:
- Every extraction run produces: confirmed skills (reinforce), new candidates (queue), co-occurrence observations (edge weights)
- Curation processes these signals and decides: accept, review, or reject
- Feedback loop: better graph → better extraction → more/better signals → better graph

## Seam: Curation ↔ Alignment (Epic 3)

Alignment events also produce signals:
- When alignment finds a sibling/parent match instead of a direct match, that reinforces the relationship edge
- When alignment consistently fails to find matches in a domain, that signals the graph has gaps in that area
- Alignment gap reports can trigger 'explore this skill cluster' curation tasks

## Absorbs Existing Issues

- forge-fi01 (Skills public/private flag)

## Dependencies

- DEPENDS ON: Epic 1 (Graph Data Model) — needs graph to populate
- Can START seeding after Epic 1 schema stable (doesnt need extraction pipeline)
- BLOCKS: nothing directly, but graph quality drives extraction + alignment quality

## Worktree Isolation

Suggested worktree: .claude/worktrees/skill-curation
- Touches: packages/core/src/services/skill-curation/, data/ (seed data), scripts/ (importers)
- Safe to parallelize with: Epics 2, 3, 5, UI, extension
- Safe to parallelize with Epic 1 AFTER schema stable
- Seeding scripts are standalone — highly parallelizable
- NOT safe to parallelize with: other work writing to skill tables concurrently

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] ESCO taxonomy imported (top 3+ levels of hierarchy)
- [ ] O*NET skills imported and cross-referenced with ESCO
- [ ] Existing ~200 Forge skills mapped into seeded taxonomy
- [ ] LLM curation produces edge proposals with confidence scores
- [ ] Human review queue surfaces proposals above threshold
- [ ] Extraction events update node confidence scores
- [ ] New skill candidates from HNSW misses appear in review queue
- [ ] Co-occurrence edge weights update from JD processing with temporal windowing
- [ ] Duplicate detection prevents nodes within embedding distance threshold
notes: DOCUMENTATION REQUIREMENT: All work under this epic MUST update docs/src/architecture/graphs/skills.md (curation section) and docs/src/architecture/graphs/computed.md (market stats pipeline). Document seeding methodology, quality gates, and growth loop metrics.

title: Qualifications
description: Qualifications domain: credentials (clearances, licenses), certifications (skill-validating certs), qualification schemas, data migration from legacy source_clearances, and IR compiler integration.

title: Skills
description: Skills domain: skill CRUD, categories, sub-graph relationships (parent/child/alias/sibling), extraction without MCP, deduplication, taxonomy/ontology design, alternative spellings, skill levels, and cross-entity clickable references.

title: 3D/Embeddings Viz
description: Interactive 3D visualizations for high-dimensional data: UMAP skill cluster exploration, role similarity mapping, JD clustering. Requires embedding computation and 3D rendering (three.js, deck.gl).

title: Graph Views
description: Graph-based visualizations: Obsidian-style link maps, skills clusters, bullets mindmap/sankey, edge rendering, node labels, filters, search, and toolbar. Built on existing GraphView component.

title: Opportunity Map
description: Geographic opportunity visualization: choropleth maps, hexagonal binning by zip/city, drill-down scopes (country to state to region to city), and location data enrichment.

title: Dashboard Framework
description: Tabbed dashboard interface, responsive layouts for web/mobile/desktop/TUI distributions, dashboard navigation, and shared dashboard infrastructure.

title: Gantt Chart
description: Application timeline Gantt chart visualization: data quality, rendering, interaction (tooltips, click nav, zoom), and usability improvements.

title: Cx Support
description: Customer support tooling, help systems, feedback channels, issue reporting flows, and support workflow automation for Forge SaaS users.

title: Infrastructure
description: Deployment and infrastructure management: Terraform, Cloudflare, Docker/K8s, edge computing, CDN, 3rd-party services, SaaS infrastructure, and environment management.

title: Observability
description: Monitoring, alerting, distributed tracing, metrics, structured logging, OTEL collectors, Grafana dashboards, and incident response tooling. Covers both infrastructure and application instrumentation.

title: Compliance
description: Legal compliance, data privacy (GDPR, CCPA), terms of service, SOC2 readiness, and regulatory requirements for operating Forge as a SaaS product.

title: Security (Infrastructure)
description: Infrastructure-level security: WAF, secrets management, network policies, TLS, container security scanning, supply chain security. NOT application-level security (that's Core Platform).

title: CI/CD
description: Continuous integration and deployment pipelines for all Forge packages. Build, test, lint, publish workflows. Covers GitHub Actions, artifact management, and deployment automation.

title: Channels
description: Distribution channels for Forge: web app, desktop (Tauri/Dioxus), mobile, browser extension stores (Chrome/Firefox/Safari), package registries, and self-hosted/OSS distribution.

title: Release Planning
description: Release cadence, milestone management, release notes, and go/no-go criteria for shipping Forge updates across all distribution channels.

title: Versioning
description: Version strategy across all Forge packages and distributions. Semantic versioning, changelog generation, version synchronization across monorepo packages.

title: Feature Flagging
description: Feature flag infrastructure for gradual rollouts, per-tenant flags, and A/B testing. Covers flag service evaluation, SDK integration, and flag management workflows.

title: Test Coverage Retrofit
description: Backfilling acceptance, component, and contract test coverage for already-shipped phases. Systematic audit of coverage gaps and prioritized test authoring.

title: Benchmarks
description: Performance and comparison benchmarks across TeX crates, graph databases, vector search, hybrid databases, SQL/edge databases, and visualization libraries. Structured evaluation harnesses for technology selection.

title: Test Infrastructure
description: CI/CD test pipelines, test harnesses, fixture automation, dev-mode capture tooling, and test runner configuration. The tooling that makes tests run reliably and fast.

title: Testing Strategy
description: Overarching testing approach, test case goals, coverage targets, and methodology decisions across test kinds (unit, component, regression, integration, E2E, visual, contract). Defines what we test, why, and how.

title: UI/UX
description: Conceptual and strategic UI/UX work: branding, accessibility, internationalization, design systems, component architecture, polish and consistency, and user story definition. Informs Editor Interfaces but operates at the cross-cutting/strategic level.

title: Editor Interfaces
description: Implementation of editor/builder UIs for specific entity types: Resume Builder, User Profile, Summaries, Job Descriptions, and Experience Views. Each interface owns its page components, forms, and interaction patterns.

title: Core Integrations
description: External system connections in forge-* packages: LinkedIn, Workday, Greenhouse, Indeed, Email, JobSpy (job board crawling), and CalDAV (calendar integration). Each integration handles data sync, automation, and profile management.

title: AI Engineering
description: AI/ML capabilities spanning development and operations: MLOps (SLM/fine-tuned models), LLMOps (prompt management, variablization, platform evaluation), NLP and Extraction (skill extraction, dedup, taxonomy), Embeddings and Similarity, and AI Gateway (multi-provider LLM routing).

title: Core Platform
description: Cross-cutting platform capabilities in forge-* packages: application security (OWASP, input validation, CORS), authentication and identity, and SaaS infrastructure (multi-tenancy, rate limiting, data isolation). Changes to forge-* packages.

title: Core Implementations
description: Domain-specific functionality in forge-* packages. Each initiative owns its implementations, schemas, data models, and semantic models. Covers Skills, Qualifications, Organizations, Resumes, Compensation and Benefits, Contacts, Events, JDs, Roles, and Bullets.

title: Browser Extensions
description: Cross-browser extension (Chrome, Firefox, Safari) for job capture, field filling, and Forge integration. Encompasses extraction plugins, autofill, JD parsing, content scripts, side panel UI, and extension-specific security.

title: Dashboard & Visualization
description: Data visualization surfaces in Forge: Gantt charts, opportunity maps, graph views, 3D embedding visualizations, and the dashboard framework (tabbed interface, responsive layouts across web/mobile/desktop/TUI).

title: Operations
description: Infrastructure, security, compliance, observability, and customer support — everything OUTSIDE forge-* application code. Deployment (Terraform, Cloudflare, Docker/K8s, edge, CDN), monitoring, alerting, SaaS infra, and 3rd-party service management. NOT changes to forge-* packages.

title: Rust Rewrite
description: Incremental TS-to-Rust migration across R0 (core types + SDK), R1 (HTTP server), R2 (Tauri desktop), R3 (MCP server port), and R4 (Dioxus UI). Strangler fig pattern — new Rust code runs alongside existing TS until parity.

title: Documentation
description: User-facing docs, API reference, and architecture documentation for Forge. Covers onboarding guides, SDK docs, architectural decision records, and internal technical documentation.

title: Developer Experience
description: Developer-facing tooling and workflow quality: pre-commit hooks, doctests, linting (ast-grep), code health, local dev ergonomics, and contributor onboarding. Everything that makes working on Forge faster and safer.

title: Distribution
description: Release management, feature flagging, versioning strategy, distribution channels, and CI/CD pipelines for delivering Forge as SaaS + OSS across web, desktop, mobile, and extension targets.

title: Testing & QA
description: Quality assurance strategy, infrastructure, benchmarks, and coverage across all Forge packages. Encompasses testing methodology, CI/CD test pipelines, benchmark harnesses, and retrofitting coverage for shipped phases.

title: Application Timeline Dashboard: GANTT chart is unusable
description: The Application Timeline Dashboard is supposed to be a GANTT chart, but the usability is effectively zero. Chart interactions, rendering, and data mapping all need significant work. This will likely require a full Epic for troubleshooting, refactoring, and refining the GANTT implementation.

title: Dashboard UI/UX: tabbed interface for multiple dashboards
description: Review and refactor the Forge dashboard UI/UX. Should be a tabbed interface for different dashboard views (Overview, Opportunity Map, Timeline, Skills, etc.). Also need to consider how dashboards will be represented across distributions: Web, Mobile, Desktop, TUI. Design for responsive/adaptive layouts.

title: Review drifted entities detection logic and semantics
description: Need to review how 'drifted entities' are detected across the system. What does drift mean in each context? When is drift expected vs problematic? Current detection may have false positives or miss real drift. Need to define drift semantics clearly and ensure detection is accurate and actionable.

title: Review and document User Stories for interacting with Forge
description: Need to review what the user stories for interacting with Forge look like across real-world scenarios. Examples: (1) Recruiter from unknown org reaches out via email with JD, (2) Known recruiter on LinkedIn shares new role without JD, (3) Found a role on Indeed and know someone at the company, (4) Friend told me about an unposted position on their team. These stories should inform UX flows and feature priorities.

title: Resume Certifications Section: entries need search/dropdown instead of radio buttons
description: Resume Certifications Section entries are currently selected via radio-button page selection. This is cumbersome when there are many certifications. Need to replace with a search/dropdown selection pattern for faster, more intuitive certification selection.

title: Resume LaTeX/Markdown editor: persistent formatting without breaking Structured IR
description: Resume LaTeX Editor views need to support persistently adding formatting like line-breaks and spacing without breaking the Structured IR. Same issue applies to the Markdown Editor view. Currently, manual formatting tweaks are lost or corrupt the IR on re-render. Need a formatting layer that persists alongside the structured data.

title: Resume Certifications Section: vendors need DnD reordering
description: Resume Certifications Section vendors (e.g. 'AWS', 'ISC2', 'CompTIA') cannot be reordered. Need drag-and-drop reordering support to let users prioritize certification vendors for a specific resume.

title: Resume Skills Section: entries need search/dropdown instead of radio buttons
description: Resume Skills Section entries are currently selected via radio-button page selection. This is cumbersome for large skill lists. Need to replace with a search/dropdown selection pattern for faster, more intuitive skill entry selection.

title: Resume Skills Section: categories need DnD reordering
description: Resume Skills Section categories (e.g. 'Programming Languages', 'Cloud Platforms', etc.) cannot be reordered. Need drag-and-drop reordering support to let users prioritize skill categories for a specific resume.

title: Resume: show Location at Role-level with optional metadata override
description: Resumes need to show Location at the Role level, with the ability to optionally override or selectively prioritize metadata like '1B471' (AFSC) or 'E5' (rank) instead of location. The Org-level section should support optional metadata labels, e.g.: 'United States Air Force':'Reserve', 'United States Marine Corps':'Active', 'Cisco':'Contract', etc.

title: Comprehensive compensation automation and normalization
description: Need comprehensive automation for capturing arbitrary compensation values and translating them into a preferred normalized view. Must handle: Currency conversion, CompensationTimeUnit (hourly/monthly/annual), CompensationRhythm (bi-weekly/monthly). Must be Base vs TotalComp vs OTE aware, with support for Vesting Schedules, Option Schedules, Bonuses, Sign-on bonuses, RSUs, and stock options.

title: Location type: support Remote/Hybrid/On-Site variants tied to OrgCampus
description: The Location type needs updating to support variants for Remote/Hybrid/On-Site work modes. These need to tie into OrgCampus values so that a role's location can express 'Remote from anywhere', 'Hybrid at Campus X', or 'On-Site at Campus Y'. Schema change + migration required.

title: JD Description: automate section identification from raw text
description: The JD Description field needs automation for identifying text sections from pasted job descriptions. Sections to detect: RoleResponsibilities, RoleReqsHard, RoleReqsSoft, RolePreferences, RoleDescriptionProse, RoleTeamDescription, RoleSalaryRange, RoleLearnMore, OrgAboutUs, OrgBenefits, OrgDisclaimers, etc. This enables structured data extraction from unstructured JD text.

title: Location entry: autocomplete with Remote/Hybrid/On-Site toggles
description: Location entry components should provide autocomplete/suggestive input with toggles for Remote/Hybrid/On-Site work modes. Location suggestions should be informed by the selected/related Organization and its known campuses. Reduces manual entry and ensures location data consistency.

title: Multi-line text entry: Markdown syntax highlighting + preview/edit toggle
description: All multi-line text entry components should support Markdown syntax highlighting by default, with a preview/edit toggle for showing rendered Markdown. This applies across the app: JD descriptions, notes, bullet text, summary fields, etc. Need a shared MarkdownEditor component.

title: Search/add skills dropdown doesn't dismiss on click-away
description: The 'search or add skills' search/dropdown component doesn't close when clicking elsewhere on the page. It only dismisses if you complete the 'Add skill' flow. Expected: clicking outside the dropdown should close it. Currently requires a page reload to dismiss.

title: Skills sub-graph structure + clickable skill references from all sources
description: Skills need more structure with a sub-graph for tracking relationships (parent/child, related, prerequisite). All Skill references across the UI (JD required/preferred, Resume skills, Bullet tags, gap analysis) should be clickable, opening a Skill modal/detail view. This enables cross-cutting skill exploration from any context.

title: Review 'extract skills' process for MCP-free reliability
description: Review the extract skills process to make it reliable without requiring an MCP client. Brainstorm approaches: can skill extraction be fully programmatic? Do we need an SLM or fine-tuned model for data extraction that can be embedded in Forge? Explore options for local/embedded skill extraction that doesn't depend on external LLM tooling.

title: JD view: linked resumes should be clickable links to Resume page
description: Linked resumes on the JD view need to be clickable links that navigate to the Resume page, similar to how the Resume view 'linked JDs' section has clickable links to the JD View. Consistent cross-linking between related entities.

title: JD view: save/delete buttons are UI inconsistent
description: The save and delete buttons on the JD view page are visually inconsistent with the rest of the UI. Need to align with shared button component patterns (btn-primary, btn-danger, btn-ghost from base.css).

title: R1: Storage backend trait abstraction
description:
Design and implement a trait boundary between stores and database backends in forge-sdk. Stores call through the trait; backends (sqlite, turso, helixdb) implement it.

Depends on forge-58ht (DB strategy decision) to know which backends exist and whether they share a SQL-compatible interface or need entity-level abstraction.

Approach options (to be decided after forge-58ht):
A. SQL executor trait — stores keep their SQL, swap the executor. Works if all backends speak SQLite-compatible SQL.
B. Entity-level trait — matches the TS StorageAdapter interface (create/get/list/update/delete on entity type + data). Required for non-SQL backends.
C. Store trait per entity — trait per domain entity. Most flexible, largest surface area.

Reference: TS adapter design at .claude/plans/forge-resume-builder/refs/specs/2026-04-09-phase0-adapter-interface-design.md

Blocked by: forge-58ht (DB strategy), rename refactor (forge-sdk stores restructure).

title: R1: Refactor forge-sdk — rename repos to stores, extract sqlite backend
description:
Pure structural refactor of forge-sdk's data access layer. No behavior change.

1. Rename: db/*_repo.rs → db/stores/*.rs (drop the _repo suffix, e.g. bullet_repo.rs → stores/bullet.rs)
2. Create: db/backends/sqlite.rs — move rusqlite Connection wiring into it
3. Update: db/mod.rs re-exports, Forge facade, and all forge-server route imports
4. Update: forge-server routes that import from forge_sdk::db::*Repository → forge_sdk::db::stores::*

No trait abstraction yet — that depends on the DB strategy decision (forge-58ht). This is folder reorganization + making the backend boundary visible in the module tree.

Current state: 12 repo files with 136 direct conn: &Connection calls. All tests (265) must continue passing.
notes: TS baseline commit: 701260c. To check for TS drift: git diff 701260c..HEAD -- packages/core/src/services/ packages/core/src/types/
close_reason: Renamed 11 repo files to stores/, normalized struct names to *Store, created backends/sqlite.rs seam. All 265 tests passing. Committed as 905faa8.

title: R1: OpenFeature SDK integration for Rust
description:
Integrate the OpenFeature SDK into forge-server as the vendor-neutral feature flag interface. OpenFeature provides a standard API so the backend provider (Flipt, LaunchDarkly, etc.) can be swapped without code changes.

Implementation:
- Add openfeature-rust SDK to forge-server
- Create a provider adapter for the selected remote flag service
- Wire into Axum state (flag client available in handlers)
- Define flag evaluation context (tenant_id, user_id, environment)
- Expose flag state to route handlers via extractor or state

Depends on: remote flag service selection (to know which provider to implement).

title: Research — remote feature flag service evaluation
description:
Evaluate remote feature flag services for Forge SaaS. Need per-tenant targeting, gradual rollouts, kill switches.

Candidates:
- Flipt (OSS, self-hosted, gRPC/REST API, Rust SDK via openfeature-flipt)
- LaunchDarkly (managed, mature Rust SDK, expensive at scale)
- Unleash (OSS, self-hosted, has Rust SDK)
- GrowthBook (OSS, self-hosted, no official Rust SDK — REST API only)
- Flagsmith (OSS, self-hosted, REST API)
- DevCycle (managed, OpenFeature native)

Evaluation criteria:
- Rust SDK maturity and maintenance
- OpenFeature compatibility (vendor-neutral standard)
- Self-hosted option (dogfooding + cost control)
- Per-user/tenant targeting
- Cost at small scale (< 1000 MAU)
- Operational complexity of self-hosting
- Webhook/SSE for real-time flag updates vs polling

title: Decision — multi-tenant database strategy
description:
Select the database strategy for Forge SaaS multi-tenancy AND inform the storage backend trait shape for forge-sdk.

## Current State

Single SQLite file (data/forge.db), rusqlite with Mutex<Forge>. 12 store modules with 136 direct conn: &Connection calls. All SQL is rusqlite-native.

## Multi-Tenancy Options

(1) SQLite-per-tenant — each tenant gets their own .db file. Simple isolation, scales horizontally. Complexity: connection management, migrations across N databases, backup per-tenant.
(2) Shared Postgres — single database, tenant_id column on every table. Standard SaaS pattern. Complexity: query scoping, migration from SQLite schema, connection pooling (sqlx or deadpool-postgres).
(3) Turso/libSQL — SQLite-compatible, edge-native, per-tenant databases as a service. Schema-per-tenant with platform-managed replication. Most natural migration path from current SQLite.
(4) Schema-per-tenant Postgres — each tenant gets a Postgres schema. Middle ground.
(5) Hybrid — SQLite for local/self-hosted, Postgres/Turso for cloud SaaS.

## Trait Abstraction Approaches (informs forge-txdt)

The Rust forge-sdk needs a trait boundary between stores and backends. The right trait shape depends on what the second backend looks like. Three options:

(A) SQL executor trait — stores keep their SQL strings, trait provides execute/query. Works if all backends speak SQLite-compatible SQL (SQLite + Turso/libSQL share the same dialect). Thinnest abstraction, lowest effort, most stores stay unchanged.
(B) Entity-level trait — create/get/list/update/delete on (entity_type, data). Matches the TS StorageAdapter/ELM design (see .claude/plans/forge-resume-builder/refs/specs/2026-04-09-phase0-adapter-interface-design.md). Required for non-SQL backends (HelixDB graph queries).
(C) Store trait per entity — trait per domain entity (BulletStore, SourceStore, etc.). Most flexible, largest surface area (~136 methods across 12 stores). Best type safety, worst maintenance burden.

## Trait Shaping Strategy

Don't design the trait from theory. Instead:
1. SQLite works now (265 tests proving the data access layer)
2. Pick the most likely second backend (probably Turso — SQLite-compatible, natural SaaS path)
3. Spike: implement 1-2 stores against the second backend. Don't ship — just learn: does the same SQL work? What's the connection model? What diverges?
4. Extract the trait from the two concrete examples — now the abstraction covers real differences, not hypothetical ones.

If SQLite and Turso share SQL → approach A (thin executor trait, minimal refactor).
If the second backend has a different query model → approach B (entity-level adapter, port the TS design).
Approach C is only justified if backends have radically different semantics per entity.

## Evaluation Criteria

- Data isolation guarantees (tenant data must never leak)
- Migration path from current rusqlite (how much SQL rewrites?)
- Operational complexity at 10/100/1000 tenants
- Backup/restore per-tenant
- Cost at small scale
- Rust ecosystem support (crate maturity, async vs sync)
- How it shapes the trait abstraction (the real downstream dependency)

## Reference

- TS adapter design: .claude/plans/forge-resume-builder/refs/specs/2026-04-09-phase0-adapter-interface-design.md
- TS HelixDB adapter: .claude/plans/forge-resume-builder/refs/specs/2026-04-14-helix-adapter-design.md
- TS storage abstraction spike: .claude/plans/forge-resume-builder/refs/specs/2026-04-08-storage-abstraction-spike-design.md
- Downstream: forge-txdt (trait abstraction), forge-49fp (rate limiting), forge-j9by (auth)

title: Multi-user authentication & authorization
description:
Design and implement multi-user auth for Forge SaaS. Currently the API has zero auth (single-user local app).

Scope:
- Auth provider selection (Auth0, Clerk, Supabase Auth, self-hosted Keycloak, OIDC)
- JWT/session validation middleware for Axum
- User model (id, email, roles, tenant_id)
- Tenant isolation at the API layer (requests scoped to authenticated tenant)
- API key support for programmatic access (MCP server, CLI, extensions)
- RBAC or simple role model (admin, user, viewer)

Dependencies: needs DB strategy decision first (where do users/tenants live?). Needs feature flag infra to gate auth behind a flag during transition.

Non-goals for v1: SSO/SAML, fine-grained permissions (resource-level ACLs), social login beyond OAuth2.
notes: Logical dependencies (can't wire cross-type): depends on forge-58ht (DB strategy — where do users/tenants live?) and forge-mgr2 (feature flag infra — gate auth during transition)

title: SaaS Infrastructure — multi-tenant platform readiness
description: Epic grouping all SaaS infrastructure work: multi-user auth, multi-tenant DB strategy, rate limiting, observability (OTEL), and feature flag infrastructure. Forge is being developed from a single-user local tool into a SaaS product. This epic tracks the platform layer that enables that transition. Not all children need to land before SaaS launch — they should be prioritized by what unblocks paying users first.
notes: Children (tracked via labels, not dep links): forge-j9by (auth), forge-58ht (DB strategy), forge-49fp (rate limiting), forge-mgr2 (feature flags), forge-mar5 (OTEL)

title: R1: forge-ai call instrumentation (latency, tokens, cost)
description: Instrument the forge-ai LLM client with tracing spans for: request latency, token usage (input/output/cache), estimated cost, model selection, retry count, error classification. These spans should flow into OTEL when enabled. Also useful standalone via tracing stdout. Pattern: wrap each API call in a tracing::instrument span with structured fields. Reference: the mitmproxy telemetry design at .claude/plans/claude-code-telemetry/ captures similar fields.

title: R1: OTEL integration — traces/metrics/logs export
description: Add OpenTelemetry support to forge-server behind a Cargo feature flag. Scope: (1) tracing-opentelemetry layer for distributed traces, (2) OTLP exporter for traces+logs, (3) Prometheus metrics endpoint or OTLP metrics export (request count, latency histograms, error rates, DB query timing). Target: Docker/K8s deployment. The tracing foundation is already in place (tracing crate + TraceLayer + X-Request-Id). Adding OTEL is swapping the subscriber. Crates: opentelemetry, opentelemetry-otlp, tracing-opentelemetry, opentelemetry-prometheus (optional). Must be feature-flagged so local dev doesn't pull in the dep tree.

title: R1: Decision — feature flag infrastructure for Rust crates
description:
Select a feature flag approach for the Rust rewrite. Forge is being developed into a SaaS product, so this needs to scale beyond a personal tool.

Three layers to evaluate:
(1) Cargo features — compile-time opt-in for heavy deps (OTEL, tectonic). This is settled (standard Rust pattern).
(2) Runtime feature flags — toggling behavior per-tenant without recompilation. Options: Flipt (OSS, self-hosted, gRPC), OpenFeature SDK (vendor-neutral standard), Unleash (OSS), GrowthBook, LaunchDarkly (managed). For SaaS: need per-tenant targeting, gradual rollouts, A/B testing capability.
(3) Build/deploy flags — env-var based, for infra-level toggles (enable OTEL, select DB backend).

Evaluation criteria: Rust SDK maturity, self-hosted option (for dogfooding), OpenFeature compatibility, per-user/tenant targeting, cost at small scale.

TS codebase uses homegrown capability detection (packages/mcp/src/utils/feature-flags.ts) — not suitable for SaaS.

title: R1: route — alignment (embedding/similarity)
description: Port packages/core/src/routes/alignment.ts to forge-server. Uses existing embedding infrastructure and existing repos. Alignment routes check resume-JD fit via cosine similarity.
notes:
**TS baseline: capture before starting.**

Before beginning the Rust port, record the TS baseline commit:
```bash
git log -1 --format="%h %s" -- packages/core/src/routes/alignment.ts packages/core/src/services/embedding-service.ts
```
Then add the result to this bead's notes as: `TS baseline commit: <hash>`

Current last-known TS commit for alignment.ts: 4c1081d

To check for TS drift after porting:
```bash
git diff <baseline>..HEAD -- packages/core/src/routes/alignment.ts packages/core/src/services/embedding-service.ts
```

Blocked on forge-ai phase 2 (embeddings + alignment algorithms).

title: R1: Remaining route modules (17 TS routes not yet ported)
description: 17 TS route files don't have Rust equivalents yet. These mostly need new repos in forge-sdk first. Categories: lookup tables (addresses, campuses, domains, industries, role-types, archetypes, certifications, credentials), services (audit, review, integrity, export, derivations, alignment), other (supporting, templates, answer-bank, extension). Priority order: audit/review/integrity (have Rust services), lookup tables (simple CRUD), then complex service routes.
notes: Parent: forge-vuc6 (R1 epic). Labels: cat:core:arch, scope:task
close_reason: Decomposed into 32 tracked beads: 3 forge-core types, 10 forge-sdk repos, 19 forge-server routes, with full dependency chains

title: R0.sdk: Resume repo + service implementation
description: Most complex domain. Section CRUD + entry CRUD + position reordering. Resume-skill junction (per-section pinned skills). Resume-certification junction. Overrides (markdown, LaTeX, summary). Gap analysis queries.
close_reason: Resume repo fully implemented with 39 passing tests. CRUD for resumes, sections, entries, skills (per-section), certifications. Plus get_with_entries hydration, gap analysis helpers, header/override storage. compile_ir removed (belongs in IR Compiler task forge-9fwl). All 134 total tests pass.

title: R0.sdk: IR Compiler — LaTeX library research + implementation
description: Research LaTeX compiler library options (typst, tectonic, latexcompile, biblatex, listings-rust, pgfplots-rs, pulldown-latex, mitex, oak-tex, rustex, latex-rs, texcore). Compare: feature gaps/overlaps, reliability/maturity, performance, complexity. Then implement: compile resume data→ResumeDocument IR tree, section-type dispatch, profile merge into header, LaTeX+Markdown rendering.
close_reason: IR Compiler fully implemented with 11 passing tests. compile() builds ResumeDocument IR from DB (header/profile merge, summary resolution, 8 section-type builders). render_markdown() and render_latex() produce complete documents. 166 total tests pass. R0.sdk epic 9/9 complete.

title: R0.sdk: Support services (Audit, Review, Integrity, Export)
description: Cross-cutting services. Audit: chain tracing (perspective→bullet→source), snapshot integrity. Review: pending queue (in_review bullets+perspectives). Integrity: content drift detection. Export: JSON/LaTeX/PDF, data bundle, DB backup via rusqlite API.
close_reason: All 4 support services implemented with 21 passing tests. AuditService (chain tracing + integrity checking), ReviewService (pending queue), IntegrityService (drift detection), ExportService (data bundles + SQL dump). 155 total tests pass.

title: R0.sdk: JD + Contact + Note + Profile domains
description: Four independent CRUD domains. JD: organization FK, resume links junction, find_by_url. Contact: 3 junction tables (org, JD, resume) with typed relationships. Note: polymorphic references. Profile: singleton CRUD, address, profile_urls.
close_reason: JD repo (8 tests): CRUD, find_by_url, org name hydration, status/org filters. Contact repo (7 tests): CRUD, 3 junction tables (org/JD/resume), reverse lookups. Note repo (12 tests): CRUD, polymorphic references, search. Profile repo (7 tests): singleton CRUD, address upsert, URL array replace.

title: R0.sdk: Perspective repo + service implementation
description: Depends on bullet. Status transitions + rejection reason. get_with_chain() joining perspective→bullet→source. Filter by archetype, domain, framing, source_id.
close_reason: Perspective repo: CRUD, get_with_chain (3-table join), status transitions, archetype/domain/framing filters, 5 tests

title: R0.sdk: Source repo + service implementation
description: First real CRUD implementation establishing the repo pattern. Extension tables (source_roles, source_projects, source_education, source_presentations). Composite unique constraint (title, organization_id). Status transitions draft→approved→deriving. Cascade delete source→delete child bullets.
close_reason: Source repo implemented with real rusqlite: CRUD for base + 4 extension tables, list with filters (type/status/search) + pagination, 9 tests passing against in-memory SQLite

title: R0.sdk: Bullet repo + service implementation
description: Depends on source. Junction tables: bullet_sources, bullet_skills (technologies). Status transitions: draft→in_review→approved/rejected. Create from derivation output.
close_reason: Bullet repo: CRUD, status transitions (draft→in_review→approved/rejected), technologies via bullet_skills junction, source linking, 7 tests

title: R0.sdk: Skill + Organization + Summary domains
description: Three independent CRUD domains. Skill: get-or-create, domain junction, merge, category. Organization: tags (JSON array), aliases, locations. Summary: industry/role_type FKs, skill keywords, clone.
close_reason: Skill repo (12 tests): CRUD, get-or-create, domain junction, merge across 8 tables, case-insensitive lookup. Org repo (10 tests): CRUD, tags junction, alias search, paginated list with filters. Summary repo (12 tests): CRUD, toggle template, clone, skill keywords, linked resume count.

title: R0.sdk: Foundation — DB connection, migrations, Forge facade
description: Connection wrapper around rusqlite::Connection. Migration runner embedding 49 SQL files via include_str!, tracking in _migrations table. Forge struct as public facade constructing all services. Forge::open(path) and Forge::open_memory() for tests. Transaction helper.
close_reason: Connection wrapper, 49 embedded SQL migrations via include_str!, Forge::open()/open_memory() facade, WAL+FK pragmas, 5 tests passing

title: R0: Common utilities (ID gen, timestamps)
description: Add src/util.rs to forge-core: new_id() -> String (uuid v4), now_iso() -> String (ISO 8601 UTC timestamp). Add uuid dep to forge-core Cargo.toml. These are used by every repository in forge-sdk for insert operations.
close_reason: util.rs with new_id() and now_iso(), uuid+chrono deps, 3 passing tests

title: R0: Serde conformance tests
description: Write #[cfg(test)] tests verifying: enum round-trip (serialize/deserialize), tagged union discrimination (IRSectionItem, Gap, SourceExtension), Option<Option<T>> null-clearing in update inputs, JSON shapes match TS wire format. This is the verification gate for forge-core before R0.sdk begins.
close_reason: 24 tests: enum roundtrip (11), tagged unions (4), error serialization (3), entity serde (1), util (3), invalid parse (1). All pass.

title: R0: Default derives for input types
description: Add #[derive(Default)] to all Update* and Create* input types in inputs.rs. Enables struct update syntax for callers. Review fields needing non-Default values (e.g. country_code defaults to US). Verify Default + serde skip_serializing_if patterns work together.
close_reason: All Update* types already had Default from stubs. Create* types correctly lack Default (required fields).

title: R0: Enum Display + FromStr for DB column mapping
description: Add Display and FromStr impls to all 30+ enums in enums.rs. SQLite stores enum values as snake_case text — these impls bridge typed Rust enums and DB TEXT columns. Consider strum crate macros vs hand-written. Must round-trip: display then parse returns original value.
close_reason: strum Display/EnumString/AsRefStr on all 30 enums, snake_case round-trip verified

title: R0: ForgeError thiserror enum
description: Replace ForgeError struct with thiserror enum. Variants: NotFound, Validation, Conflict, ForeignKey, Database(rusqlite::Error), Internal. Keep Serialize impl emitting {code,message,details} JSON for API compat. Remove ForgeResult/PaginatedResult enums (use std Result). Update all forge-sdk stub return types. Add From<rusqlite::Error> via thiserror #[from].
close_reason: ForgeError thiserror enum with 6 variants, Serialize impl for API compat, removed ForgeResult/PaginatedResult

title: R4: Workspace Preparation
description: Prepare the workspace and development environment for R4 (Dioxus UI (web + desktop + mobile)). Configure the crate, set up dependencies, wire build scripts, create initial module structure, and verify the dev loop (edit → build → test → run) works end-to-end with a minimal hello-world.
design:
Tasks:
- Cargo.toml with pinned dependency versions (from Research findings)
- Module structure matching the SPEC
- Build script (build.rs) if needed
- Dev configuration (env vars, config files)
- Verify: cargo build, cargo test, cargo run all work
- Integration with existing workspace (forge-core, forge-sdk deps)
- .gitignore updates for Rust artifacts
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Crate compiles with all dependencies
- [ ] Module structure matches SPEC
- [ ] Hello-world binary/test runs successfully
- [ ] Dev loop verified (edit → build → test → run)
- [ ] Integrated with Cargo workspace
- [ ] No conflicts with existing TS build

title: R4: Plan & Review
description: Create the implementation plan for R4 (Dioxus UI (web + desktop + mobile)). Break the SPEC into phased work items, define milestones, identify the critical path, and review the plan for feasibility. Output: ordered task list as beads with dependencies.
design:
Output:
- Phased implementation plan (ordered task beads)
- Milestone definitions with exit criteria
- Critical path identification
- Effort estimates per phase
- Review checkpoint schedule

Approach:
- Decompose SPEC into implementable chunks
- Identify what can parallelize
- Define integration test checkpoints
- Plan for incremental deployment (not big-bang)
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Implementation phases defined as child beads
- [ ] Dependencies between phases documented
- [ ] Milestones with exit criteria
- [ ] Critical path identified
- [ ] Plan reviewed against SPEC for completeness
- [ ] No phase exceeds ~1 week of work

title: R4: Research & Explore
description: Research and explore the ecosystem, frameworks, and crate landscape for R4 (Dioxus UI (web + desktop + mobile)). Evaluate framework options, study prior art, identify blockers and risks. Exit criterion: all repository and service tests pass against real SQLite, IR compiler produces identical LaTeX output to TS version.
close_reason: All 9 child tasks complete. forge-sdk crate fully implemented: 9 domain repos, 4 support services, IR compiler with LaTeX+Markdown rendering. 166 tests passing against real SQLite.

title: R0.ai: forge-ai — LLM client implementation
description:
Implement the forge-ai crate: prompt construction, response validation, embedding computation, and JD parsing. This crate does NOT call LLM APIs directly — Forge uses a split-handshake model where the server prepares prompts and the MCP client invokes the LLM. Direct API calls are deferred to the AI Gateway initiative (forge-9s98).

Responsibilities:
1. Prompt templates — render structured prompts for source→bullet, bullet→perspective, JD skill extraction
2. Response validators — strict JSON schema validation for each derivation type
3. Embedding computation — all-MiniLM-L6-v2 (384-dim), cosine similarity, compiled-in (no subprocess)
4. JD requirement parsing — section detection, requirement splitting, confidence scoring
5. Alignment algorithms — cosine similarity matrix, requirement matching with thresholds, verdict classification

Does NOT include:
- Anthropic/OpenAI API client (→ forge-9s98 AI Gateway)
- Provider routing/selection
- API key management

Exit criterion: all 3 prompt templates render correctly, all 3 validators pass/reject test cases, embedding model loads and produces correct-dimension vectors, JD parser handles structured and unstructured postings.
close_reason: Phase 1 complete: 3 prompt templates (source→bullet, bullet→perspective, JD skill extraction), 3 response validators (bullet, perspective, skill extraction), JD requirement parser (1:1 TS port). 62 tests. Phase 2 (embeddings + alignment) deferred.

title: Design: Rust implementation phases, seams, and migration strategy
description: After stubs exist, plan the implementation order within R0. Identify which domains to implement first (IR compiler recommended — pure computation, easily testable). Map the seams between crates: how forge-sdk exposes its API to forge-server, how forge-ai integrates without circular deps. Update stub doc comments with implementation notes. Create child beads for each implementation batch.
design:
Key questions to resolve:
1. Implementation order: which domain first? IR compiler is pure computation. Source/Bullet are simple CRUD. Resume is most complex.
2. Testing strategy: unit tests per service, integration tests against real SQLite. Test fixtures reusable from TS?
3. DB migration: share schema with TS (same migrate.ts output) or Rust-native migrations?
4. SDK API surface: does forge-sdk export a single Forge struct (facade) or individual services?
5. Error types: ForgeError enum shared across crates or per-crate errors?
6. Seam between sdk and server: axum extractors wrapping sdk types directly?
acceptance_criteria:

- [ ] Crate seam interfaces sketched (trait boundaries)
- [ ] Testing strategy defined
- [ ] Migration strategy defined (shared schema vs separate)

title: Research: Rust crate selection for forge-sdk and forge-ai
description: Evaluate and select Rust crates for key dependencies. Domains: SQLite access (sqlx vs rusqlite vs diesel), LaTeX/PDF compilation (tectonic vs pdflatex subprocess vs typst), LLM client (anthropic-rs vs raw reqwest), async runtime (tokio vs none for library code), serialization patterns (serde best practices for SQLite row mapping). Output: ADR per decision with rationale, benchmarks where relevant.
design:
Research areas:
1. SQLite: sqlx (async, compile-time checked) vs rusqlite (sync, mature, lighter). Consider: forge is single-user, async may be unnecessary overhead.
2. LaTeX: tectonic (pure Rust, no system deps) vs pdflatex subprocess (requires TeX install). Tectonic is ~200MB but self-contained.
3. LLM: anthropic-rs crate (if exists and maintained) vs raw reqwest + serde. Check crate maturity.
4. Async: tokio everywhere vs sync core + async only at server boundary. Library code (forge-sdk) may not need async.
5. Error handling: thiserror vs anyhow vs custom enum. Probably thiserror for library, anyhow for binaries.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] ADR written for each decision area
- [ ] Prototype code (if needed) to validate compatibility
- [ ] Cargo.toml dependency versions pinned
- [ ] Any crate compatibility issues documented
close_reason: ADR with 10 decisions: rusqlite, reqwest LLM wrapper, pdflatex, figment, thiserror/anyhow, tracing, uuid. Cargo.toml deps updated.

title: WebUI: resume status transitions

title: WebUI: resume status display and filtering

title: MCP tool: forge_update_resume_status
description: Create a new MCP tool forge_update_resume_status that wraps the repository status transition method. Accept resume ID and target status as parameters. title: Repository: status transition methods
description: Resume status transitions currently have no validation — status is set directly via update(). Add state machine validation for the extended 7-status lifecycle: backlog→draft, draft→in_review, in_review→approved, in_review→rejected, rejected→draft (rework), approved→submitted, submitted→archived, any→archived. Reject invalid transitions with error explaining allowed moves from current state. Follow the bullet/perspective pattern where approve/reject/reopen are explicit methods.

title: Migration: extend resume status CHECK to add backlog and submitted

Default remains 'draft'. Existing resumes are unaffected.

title: Stub: forge-sdk — IR Compiler
description: Stub the resume IR compiler pipeline. The compiler transforms resume data (sections, entries, perspectives, skills) into an intermediate representation, then renders to LaTeX/PDF. Most complex service in the codebase.
design:
TS Source:
- packages/core/src/services/resume-compiler-service.ts

Key behaviors:
- Fetch resume with all nested data (header, sections, entries, perspectives)
- Build IR: normalized tree structure independent of output format
- Render IR to LaTeX (template-based)
- LaTeX to PDF (subprocess: pdflatex/tectonic)
- Profile data merged into header
- Skill categories grouped and ordered per resume settings
- Section ordering preserved
- Handles missing data gracefully (empty sections omitted)

Rust divergences:
- IR as a Rust enum tree (not JSON objects)
- LaTeX escaping as a pure function
- PDF subprocess via std::process::Command
- This is the best candidate for first real implementation (R0) — pure computation, easily testable

title: R3: MCP Server Port (TS→Rust)
description: Port MCP server from TS to Rust. MCP protocol is JSON-RPC over stdio/HTTP — straightforward to implement. forge-core already has all service logic; MCP server is a thin tool-registration layer. Eliminates last TS dependency for core stack. Ships: single Rust binary for API + MCP.

title: R4: Dioxus UI (web + desktop + mobile)
description:
R4: Dioxus replaces Svelte as the UI layer across web, desktop, and mobile. The data layer (forge-wasm) is already built and tested by this point — R4 is a UI swap, not a full rewrite.

## Revised Scope (browser-first architecture)

Original scope assumed Dioxus was a full rewrite of everything. With the forge-wasm crate (R1.5) handling all data operations, R4 scope NARROWS to:

- Replace Svelte components with Dioxus components
- Dioxus calls forge-wasm natively (no wasm-bindgen FFI, direct Rust calls)
- Cross-platform: web (WASM) + desktop (native) + mobile (native) from one Dioxus codebase
- Same data layer, same extraction pipeline, same alignment scoring — just different UI rendering

## What R4 Does NOT Need to Build

- Data layer (forge-wasm — done at R1.5)
- Storage adapters (BrowserStore, RemoteStore — done at R1.5)
- Extraction pipeline (done at Skill Epic 2)
- Alignment scoring (done at Skill Epic 3)
- CRDT sync (done at SaaS epic)
- Graph runtime (done at forge-wasm)

## What R4 DOES Build

- Dioxus component library matching current Svelte shared components
- Page-level Dioxus implementations for all Forge views
- Dioxus routing
- Dioxus state management consuming forge-wasm API
- Platform-specific adaptations (mobile layout, desktop menus)
- Accessibility (port from Svelte a11y work)

## Dependencies

- DEPENDS ON: forge-6z5l (R1.5 forge-wasm) — data layer must be stable
- R2 (Tauri) should ship first — validates Rust backend + Svelte frontend works, de-risks R4

title: R0: forge-core — production-ready types and shared traits
description: Take the forge-core stubs from dw4 and make them production-ready. Finalize serde derives, validation traits, Display/FromStr implementations, builder patterns where needed. This is the foundation crate — no I/O, no business logic, just the shared vocabulary. Exit criterion: forge-core published as a workspace crate that forge-sdk and forge-ai can depend on with stable types.
close_reason: forge-core production-ready: ForgeError thiserror enum, strum Display/FromStr on all enums, util (ID gen + timestamps), 24 serde conformance tests passing

title: R2: Tauri Desktop App (Rust backend + Svelte frontend)
description:
R2: Tauri desktop app with Svelte frontend and Rust backend. Tauri wraps the existing Svelte UI and uses forge-wasm for data operations.

## Revised Scope (browser-first architecture)

Tauri provides:
- Native desktop window wrapping Svelte web UI
- Rust backend via Tauri commands, consuming forge-wasm natively
- File system access (local SQLite, not wa-sqlite/OPFS)
- Desktop-specific features (system tray, native menus, auto-update)
- Same Svelte UI as web, different storage backend (native SQLite vs wa-sqlite)

## Key Integration

forge-wasm crate is consumed by Tauri's Rust backend:
- ForgeRuntime initialized with native SQLite adapter (not wa-sqlite)
- Same repos, services, extraction, alignment as browser — different storage backend
- Tauri commands expose forge-wasm API to Svelte frontend via IPC

## Dependencies

- DEPENDS ON: forge-6z5l (R1.5 forge-wasm) — data layer crate
- Validates the architecture before R4 (Dioxus)

title: R1: Rust HTTP Server — strangler fig migration
description:
R1: Axum HTTP server replacing Hono via strangler fig migration. In the browser-first architecture, this server serves THREE purposes:

1. Self-hosted OSS server (for power users who want server-side features without SaaS)
2. SaaS API server (sync endpoints, curation, analytics, 3rd-party API proxy)
3. Hono replacement for existing server-dependent workflows during migration

## Revised Context (browser-first)

The Axum server is NOT the primary data store for most users. Browser wa-sqlite (via forge-wasm) is the primary. The server provides:
- Sync endpoint for SaaS CRDT merge
- LLM curation pipeline (batch, server-side)
- Market intelligence aggregation
- Premium analytics and 3rd-party API proxy
- Self-hosted option for OSS power users

R1 work feeds into forge-wasm (R1.5): the Rust repos and services built here are the same ones compiled to WASM for browser execution. Same code, different compile target.
notes:
TS baseline convention: all child R1 beads record their TS baseline commit in notes.
To check global TS drift across all ported routes:
  git diff 701260c..HEAD -- packages/core/src/routes/ packages/core/src/services/ packages/core/src/types/

description: When a form field has FieldKind=unknown, send the label text to POST /api/extension/match-fields. An LLM maps the label to the most likely FieldKind, then the extension fills from the answer bank or profile. Handles arbitrary forms.

title: Extension: Switch to optional host_permissions for new job board sites

title: Extension: New job board plugin support (generic plugin interface validation)
description: Validate the generic JobBoardPlugin interface against a third job board (e.g., Greenhouse). Proves the interface is truly generic: same extractJD, detectFormFields, fillField contract works across different DOM structures.

title: Parser L3 extractLocations() truncates multi-word city names
description: "New York, NY" extracted as "York, NY". The City regex pattern in l3-extractors.ts allows 1-4 capitalized words but appears to match greedily from the wrong boundary. Affects campus resolution accuracy (M5b blocker). Fix in packages/core/src/parser/l3-extractors.ts.

title: Resume lifecycle statuses: backlog → draft → review → approved/rejected → submitted → archived
description:
Resumes currently have no status field — they exist as static documents with no lifecycle tracking. Add a status field that models the resume pipeline from creation through submission.

## Proposed statuses
- **backlog** — created but not actively being worked on
- **draft** — actively being built/edited
- **review** — content complete, awaiting human review
- **approved** — reviewed and ready to submit
- **rejected** — reviewed and needs rework (returns to draft)
- **submitted** — sent to employer/application
- **archived** — no longer active (position filled, withdrawn, etc.)

## Transitions
```
backlog → draft → review → approved → submitted → archived
                    ↓
                 rejected → draft (rework cycle)
```

## Impact
- Migration: add `status TEXT DEFAULT 'draft'` to resumes table + CHECK constraint
- Service: add status transition validation (e.g., can't go from backlog to submitted)
- MCP: add forge_update_resume_status tool
- UI: show status badge on resume cards, filter by status on /resumes
- Export: only allow PDF export from approved/submitted status (optional gate)
- Pairs with HITL perspective approval (forge-4g9l) — review status could gate on all perspectives being approved

title: Skill alternative spellings / match patterns
description:
Skills need alternative spellings or match patterns (regex/glob) for reliable extraction from JD text.

## Problem
JDs use inconsistent naming: "Kubernetes", "K8s", "k8s", "CI/CD", "CICD", "CI-CD", "Amazon Web Services", "AWS". The skill extractor needs to recognize all variants as the same skill.

## Proposed
Add a `skill_patterns` table:
```sql
CREATE TABLE skill_patterns (
  id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
  skill_id TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES skills(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
  pattern TEXT NOT NULL,
  pattern_type TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'literal' CHECK (pattern_type IN ('literal', 'regex', 'glob')),
  UNIQUE(skill_id, pattern)
);
```

Each skill can have multiple patterns. The extractor checks all patterns when scanning JD text. Literal patterns use case-insensitive exact match. Regex/glob for more complex cases (e.g., `CI[\/-]?CD`).

## Related
- Skill relations (aliases overlap with this — an alias is a named alternative, a pattern is a text-match rule)
- JD skill extraction pipeline
notes: Worktree: .claude/worktrees/skill-extraction/ (likely folds into the extraction pipeline epic forge-czgq — alternative-spelling matching is a pattern-based extractor concern).

title: Skill extraction should use existing skills data for matching
description:
The JD skill extraction process should be aware of the existing skills table (and skill_patterns once added) when extracting skills from job description text.

## Current state
Skill extraction produces raw text strings that are then matched or created as skills. The extractor doesn't consult the existing skills inventory, leading to duplicates and missed matches.

## Proposed
When extracting skills from a JD:
1. Load existing skills + patterns into a lookup index
2. Match JD text against known skills first (exact, alias, pattern)
3. Only create new skills for genuinely novel terms
4. Flag uncertain matches for user review

This prevents the "Docker" vs "docker" vs "Docker Engine" proliferation problem.

title: Skill relations — alias, parent, child, sibling, alternative
description:
Add a relations system to skills so they can express semantic relationships to other skills.

## Relation types
- **alias**: same skill, different name (e.g., "K8s" ↔ "Kubernetes")
- **parent**: broader category (e.g., "Kubernetes" parent of "Helm")
- **child**: inverse of parent
- **sibling**: same level, related (e.g., "Docker" ↔ "Podman")
- **alternative**: interchangeable for job matching (e.g., "AWS" ↔ "GCP" ↔ "Azure")

## Proposed schema
```sql
CREATE TABLE skill_relations (
  id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
  skill_id TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES skills(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
  related_skill_id TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES skills(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
  relation_type TEXT NOT NULL CHECK (relation_type IN ('alias', 'parent', 'child', 'sibling', 'alternative')),
  UNIQUE(skill_id, related_skill_id, relation_type)
);
```

## Impact
- Gap analysis: alias/alternative matches should count as coverage
- Skills graph: richer edges
- Dedup: alias detection during skill creation
notes: Worktree: .claude/worktrees/skill-extraction/ (likely folds into the extraction pipeline epic forge-czgq — relation discovery is a curation/extraction concern that uses the skill_graph_edges table created in forge-7rd7).

title: JD qualifications extraction — delineate certs/clearances from skills
description:
Add a qualifications dimension to job descriptions, parallel to the existing JD skills extraction. Currently all requirements are treated as skills, but certifications (Security+, CISSP) and clearances (TS/SCI) are qualifications — not skills.

## Problem
When a JD mentions "Security+" or "TS/SCI", these get extracted as skills and show up in the skills gap analysis. But they should be matched against the credentials/certifications tables, not the skills table. This conflates two different dimensions of job fit.

## Proposed
- Add a `job_description_qualifications` table (parallel to `job_description_skills`)
- Link JD qualifications to credentials/certifications for gap matching
- Update JD ingestion to classify requirements as skill vs qualification
- Update gap analysis to report qualification gaps separately from skill gaps

## Related
- Credentials + Certifications tables exist (migration 037)
- JD skills extraction: `job_description_skills` table
- Gap analysis: `forge_gap_analysis` MCP tool

title: UX for correcting a skill gap in a JD
description:
Add a user flow for resolving skill gaps identified in a JD. When the gap analysis shows a missing skill, the user needs a path to address it — either by linking an existing bullet/source that demonstrates the skill, or by acknowledging it as a true gap.

## Current state
Gap analysis shows which skills from a JD are not covered by the resume. But there is no UX to act on this — the user has to manually search for bullets, create perspectives, and add entries.

## Proposed UX
From the JD skill gap view, allow the user to:
1. Search existing bullets/perspectives that demonstrate the skill
2. Link a matching perspective to the resume (one-click add)
3. Mark a gap as "acknowledged" (true gap, no coverage exists)
4. Mark a gap as "not applicable" (skill listed in JD but not relevant)

title: JD skills UI: show Covered vs Gaps instead of full Required + Gaps
description:
Change the JD skills display in the web UI. Currently shows two lists:
- "Required Skills" (all skills from JD)
- "Skill Gaps" (subset not covered)

This is redundant — the full required list includes both covered and uncovered skills with no visual distinction.

## Proposed
Replace with a single view showing two groups:
- **Covered** — skills from the JD that are present in the linked resume (with checkmark or green indicator)
- **Gaps** — skills from the JD that are missing (with warning indicator)

This gives immediate visual feedback on coverage without needing to cross-reference two lists.

title: CI/CD pipeline for extension release automation
description:
Build a GitHub Actions (or equivalent) workflow that:
1. Triggers on version tag push (e.g., ext-v0.2.0)
2. Runs bun run build (both browsers)
3. Runs bun test (includes build output verification)
4. Packages dist/chrome/ as .zip, dist/firefox/ as .xpi
5. Publishes to Chrome Web Store via API
6. Publishes to Firefox AMO via API
7. Creates GitHub release with artifacts

Depends on: Chrome and Firefox account setup beads.

Add 'contractor' and 'recruiter' as valid options for both organization primary_type enum and tags.

Enables: location-based filtering, multi-location JD display, campus linkage.

description:
Workday multi-page application forms include 'Application Questions' and 'Voluntary Disclosures' pages that show 'No form fields found'. These pages likely use different form patterns than the standard formField-* wrappers. Need to:
- Capture fixture HTML from these pages

title: Extension: Workday dropdown filling (country code, state, custom selects)
description:
Workday uses custom dropdown widgets (button[aria-haspopup='listbox']) for country phone code, state, and other select fields. Current fillField only handles text inputs. Need to:
- Detect dropdown widgets (data-uxi-widget-type, aria-haspopup)

- Country: from profile or default

title: Extension: Inject 'Capture to Forge' sibling button via aria-label anchoring
description:
Inject a 'Capture to Forge' button next to LinkedIn's action buttons (Save, Share, More options) using aria-label selectors instead of fragile CSS class names or XPath.

Why aria-label over class selectors:
- Class names like '_8e681e09 _4e228146 f3c160da' are CSS module hashes that change on every LinkedIn CSS rebuild
- aria-label='Save' / aria-label='More options' is a semantic accessibility contract (WCAG) - changing it breaks screen readers and LinkedIn's own a11y test suite
- Orders of magnitude more stable: changes only on intentional redesign, not CSS rebuilds

Implementation:
- Find anchor: document.querySelector('button[aria-label="Save"]') or similar
- Inject sibling button styled to match LinkedIn's button aesthetic
- MutationObserver to handle SPA navigation (LinkedIn doesn't do full page reloads)
- Content script must run on LinkedIn job page load (not just on-demand)

Localization caveat: aria-label values are localized (French: 'Plus d options'). For now target English only. Future: label map per locale or fallback to aria-label existence + SVG path matching.

Fallback: If aria-label anchor not found (layout redesign), degrade gracefully to context menu only (the A dependency).
close_reason: Merged M2: injected button via aria-label

title: Extension: Chrome context menu 'Capture to Forge' on job pages
description:
Add a Chrome context menu entry ('Capture to Forge') that appears on LinkedIn job pages matching *://*.linkedin.com/jobs/*.

Uses chrome.contextMenus API - zero DOM manipulation, zero breakage risk from LinkedIn UI changes. Cross-browser compatible (Chrome + Firefox MV3). Less discoverable than an injected button but maximally robust.

Implementation:
- Register context menu in service worker on extension install
- Filter to documentUrlPatterns: ['*://*.linkedin.com/jobs/*']
- On click: trigger the same extraction + API write pipeline that the existing chip UI uses
- Show badge/notification on completion

This is the reliability baseline. The injected sibling button (aria-label approach) depends on this being in place first as a fallback.
close_reason: Merged M2: context menu capture

title: Wire JD parser into extension content scripts
description:
Integrate the JD structural parser into the extension's content script pipeline. Currently extraction is per-platform (LinkedIn plugin, Workday plugin, etc.). The parser should:

1. Run after platform-specific DOM extraction gets the raw JD text
2. Classify sections and extract structured data (salary, location, posture)
3. Feed extracted data into the chip UI and API write pipeline

This replaces ad-hoc salary/location extraction in individual plugins with a shared parser.

title: Wire JD parser into Forge core JD ingestion
description:
Integrate the JD structural parser into the Forge core JD ingestion pipeline (forge_ingest_job_description MCP tool). When a JD is ingested, automatically:

1. Parse into classified sections
2. Store section boundaries and classifications in the JD record
3. Extract and populate salary_min, salary_max, location fields automatically
4. Enable MCP tools to query specific sections (e.g., 'get requirements section for JD X')

title: L1: Heading-based section splitter
description:
Split JD raw text into sections by detecting heading patterns: markdown ## headings, HTML h1-h6 tags, bold/strong text on its own line, ALL CAPS lines, and colon-terminated labels. Output: array of { heading: string | null, content: string, startIndex: number } sections. Should handle: - Markdown: '## Responsibilities', '### Required Skills' - HTML: '

About the Role

' - Bold: '**What You Will Do**' - ALL CAPS: 'RESPONSIBILITIES' - Colon labels: 'Responsibilities:' - No heading (preamble/inline content): heading = null

title: L2: Section taxonomy classifier (keyword heuristics)
description:
Classify each section from L1 output into the taxonomy using two strategies:

1. Heading match: fuzzy match heading text against known patterns per category
2. Keyword density: for sections with no heading or ambiguous headings, count domain keywords in the content body

Taxonomy and keyword signals:
- responsibilities: 'design', 'build', 'lead', 'own', 'develop', 'implement', 'work with'
- requirements: 'experience', 'years', 'degree', 'bachelor', 'proficiency', 'knowledge of'
- preferred: 'preferred', 'nice to have', 'bonus', 'plus', 'ideally'
- description: 'about the role', 'in this role', 'you will', 'the team'
- compensation: dollar amounts, 'salary', 'base', 'equity', 'bonus', 'range'
- location: city/state patterns, 'remote', 'hybrid', 'on-site', 'office'
- benefits: '401k', 'PTO', 'insurance', 'vacation', 'leave'
- about_company: 'about us', 'who we are', 'our mission', company name patterns
- eeo: 'equal opportunity', 'affirmative action', 'disability', 'accommodation'

Output: array of { heading, content, category, confidence } with confidence: 'high' (heading match) | 'medium' (keyword) | 'low' (fallback/unclassified)

title: Inline content extractors (salary, location, work posture)
description:
Extract structured data from section content, especially from sections where the data is inline (not under its own heading). Three extractors:

1. Salary extractor: regex for dollar ranges ('$147,000-$211,000'), 'base salary range', 'compensation'. Extract min, max, currency, type (base/total/hourly).

2. Location extractor: city/state patterns ('Reston, VA'), 'Remote', 'Hybrid (IN: 3, OFF: 2)'. Handle multiple locations ('Kirkland, WA; Sunnyvale, CA'). Extract as array of { city, state, country, posture }.

3. Work posture extractor: 'Remote', 'Hybrid', 'On-site', 'In-person'. May be inline in location or in a separate 'Work Type' section.

These run after L2 classification and can cross-reference multiple sections. Salary buried in a benefits paragraph should still be found.

title: Extension: JD Structural Parser
description:
Parse job descriptions into classified sections using a layered approach: (1) heading-based splitting, (2) keyword density classification, (3) optional small-model fallback. Enables salary extraction (3bp.15), location extraction (3bp.18), and future JD analysis.

Three-layer hybrid:
- L1: Structural parser (regex) - split by headings (markdown ##, bold, ALL CAPS, HTML h-tags)
- L2: Heuristic classifier (keyword density) - classify ambiguous sections by domain keywords
- L3: Small model fallback (optional) - Qwen2.5-0.5B or fine-tuned MiniLM for unclassified sections

Should live in shared code (not extension-only) - Forge core JD ingestion can reuse the same parser.

Section taxonomy: responsibilities, requirements, preferred, description, location, compensation, benefits, about_company, eeo

title: Extension: JD location → Org Campus linkage
description:
JD location should reference an Org Campus (if org is set) rather than being a free-text string. When an org is linked:
- Match the extracted location to an existing Campus of that org
- If no match, create a new Campus
- If org is set but no campus match, set location to null (not a dangling string)
When no org is set, location stays as a plain string.

This is a data model + extension capture flow change.

title: LinkedIn plugin: location + salary chip selector drift across layouts
description: LinkedIn plugin extracts title/company/description correctly, but misses or misclassifies location and salary on the SDUI chip layout when chip order differs from the first fixture.
notes:
Reproducer: https://www.linkedin.com/jobs/view/4386859589/ (Capital One — Applied Researcher II)

Expected:
- location: 'McLean, VA' (or similar — real salary shown in description)
- salary_range: '$262,500 - $299,600' (from description body — this variant doesn't show salary as a chip at all)

Got:
- location: null
- salary_range: 'Full-time' (plugin picked up the employment-type chip, not a salary)

Root cause:
Current plugin uses svg[id='check-small'] sibling index 0 for salary and index 1 for location. On Crossing Hurdles fixture this worked because chip order was [salary, location]. On Capital One, chip order is [employment-type, workplace-type, experience-level, ...] with no salary chip at all. Location isn't a check-small chip on this page either.

Fix direction:
1. Capture Capital One page as tests/fixtures/linkedin/job-detail-capital-one.html
2. Add test case asserting expected location + salary (both can be null for this page — 'Full-time' must NOT appear as salary)
3. Rewrite chip classification: don't use positional index. Classify each chip by content pattern:
   - salary: matches /\$[\d,]+/
   - workplace: matches /Remote|Hybrid|On-site/i
   - employment: matches /Full-time|Part-time|Contract/i
   - experience: matches /Entry|Associate|Mid|Senior|Executive/i
4. Location might not be a chip at all on some layouts — need separate selectors for location (probably aria-label based, similar to company extraction)
5. Consider falling back to parsing salary from description body via regex when no salary chip found

Scope: defer until P4 (extraction -> API write) so we don't pollute the Forge DB with wrong data. P1's goal was proving the wiring; field accuracy for full layout coverage is a P4+ concern.

Blocks: P4 (extraction -> API write) — should be fixed before we start writing to Forge
close_reason: Merged in 7738f18. Chip classification rewritten from positional index to content-pattern regex. Capital One fixture + test added. 10/10 tests pass. Worktree removed.

title: Reconfigure chrome-devtools-mcp to load unpacked extensions
description: Chrome DevTools MCP currently launches Chrome with --disable-extensions which prevents testing the Forge extension via MCP automation. Reconfigure to (1) drop --disable-extensions and (2) add --load-extension=<path-to-dist> so the extension auto-loads in the MCP Chrome instance.
notes:
Motivation:
Every extension phase (P1-P7, MVP, Post-MVP) has a 'manual Chrome verification' step that blocks on human action. With MCP-loaded extensions, these become automatable via performance_start_trace, list_console_messages, list_network_requests, lighthouse_audit, etc.

Config location: chrome-devtools-mcp server launch flags. Likely in ~/.claude.json or the MCP server's own config file.

What to add:
- Remove: --disable-extensions
- Add: --load-extension=$FORGE_EXT_DIST_PATH (env var pointing to worktree dist/)
- Consider: --extensions-install-verification=... or --enable-extension-activity-logging for debugging

Limitations even after fix:
- Clicking the action icon (toolbar button) has no direct CDP API. Workarounds: open popup HTML URL as a tab, or script extraction via chrome.scripting.executeScript from a test harness.
- Service worker lifecycle via CDP is fiddly but doable.

Acceptance:
- chrome-devtools-mcp can navigate to linkedin.com/jobs/view/<id>
- list_console_messages shows no 'extension disabled' errors
- evaluate_script can call chrome.runtime.getManifest() from an extension page and get the Forge manifest

Discovered during P1 manual verification attempt (Chrome DevTools MCP launched with --disable-extensions, extension couldn't be tested in MCP Chrome, required switching to real Chrome for Task 13).
close_reason: Shipped in 38787b5. Project .mcp.json overrides plugin chrome-devtools with --category-extensions. Extensions loaded via CDP install_extension tool (--load-extension flag ineffective in Chrome Dev 149 pipe mode). Verified: list_extensions shows Forge v0.0.1 Enabled, no console errors.

title: Add MCP tools for creating and updating bullets directly
description:
Currently there is no MCP tool for manually creating bullets — only forge_derive_bullets (AI-driven) and forge_update_bullet (edit existing). Manual bullet creation requires direct SQL INSERT into bullets + bullet_sources + bullet_skills tables.

Needed tools:
1. **forge_create_bullet** — Create a bullet with content, source_id, status (default in_review), optional domain/notes/metrics. Should auto-create bullet_sources link.
2. **forge_add_bullet_skill** — Add a skill link to a bullet (bullet_skills junction)
3. **forge_remove_bullet_skill** — Remove a skill link from a bullet

Context: During the 2026-04-07 data quality audit, 12 new bullets were created for Cisco (8) and Raytheon Cloud Forensics Engineer (4) roles. All required raw SQL because no MCP tool exists for manual bullet creation. This is a common workflow when interviewing users about their experience and creating bullets collaboratively.

Note: forge_remove_resume_skill and forge_remove_resume_entry already have a void-return serialization bug (TypeError on undefined). Ensure new tools return meaningful responses.
close_reason: Already implemented in tier3-bullets.ts — forge_create_bullet, forge_add_bullet_skill, forge_remove_bullet_skill all registered and wired through SDK + routes
