Enables rapid fixture creation when a new site layout doesn't extract correctly. Output: .html file saved to clipboard or downloaded. Gated behind devMode config flag (M7).

title: Skills public/private flag — lock public skills from user edits
description:
Add a public/private flag to skills. Public skills are from a shared taxonomy (e.g., well-known technologies, certifications) and should not be renamed or recategorized by the user. Private skills are user-created and fully editable.

## Motivation
Prevents accidental edits to canonical skill names (renaming "Kubernetes" to "K8s" would break pattern matching and gap analysis). Also enables future skill sharing/import from a community taxonomy.

## Proposed
- Add `is_public BOOLEAN DEFAULT FALSE` to skills table
- Public skills: name, category, and relations are read-only via UI/API
- Users can still link/unlink public skills to bullets, resumes, etc.
- Skill creation defaults to private; import/seed scripts set public

title: Bullets mindmap/sankey visualization
description:
Add an alternative view mode to /bullets that shows bullets as a mindmap or sankey diagram. Visualize the flow from sources → bullets → perspectives, showing how content derives and branches across the pipeline.

## Motivation
The current list view makes it hard to see the derivation tree. A visual graph would help identify:
- Sources with many bullets vs few
- Bullets that have been derived into multiple perspectives
- Orphaned bullets with no perspectives
- Coverage density across domains/archetypes

title: Skills graph view — Obsidian-style link map with clusters
description:
Add a graph visualization to /skills showing skills as nodes with edges representing relationships (domains, co-occurrence in sources, category grouping). Similar to Obsidian's graph view with cluster detection.

## Desired behavior
- Nodes = skills, sized by usage frequency (bullet links, resume appearances)
- Edges = relationships (same domain, co-occurrence in bullet_sources, category)
- Clusters = skill categories or domains, visually grouped
- Interactive: click a node to see the skill detail, drag to rearrange
- Filter by category, domain, or usage threshold

## Depends on
- Skill relations (job-hunting-TBD) for richer edge types beyond co-occurrence

title: JD linked resumes should be clickable links to /resumes
description: In the JD detail view, the "Linked Resumes" section shows resume names as plain text. They should be clickable links that navigate to the resume in /resumes (ideally selecting that resume in the split panel).

title: Phase 4: DuckPGQ Adapter
description:
Implement DuckPGQ adapter for read-only analytics over existing SQLite data. SQL/PGQ (ISO SQL:2023) property graph queries via DuckDB's ATTACH mechanism — zero migration for read path. Target: analytics dashboards, coverage scoring, alignment reports.

## Context
Phase 0-2 complete. The StorageAdapter interface supports read-only adapters (AnalyticsStore pattern). DuckPGQ uses CREATE PROPERTY GRAPH over ATTACH'd SQLite tables for graph pattern matching. Key unknown: does CREATE PROPERTY GRAPH work over ATTACH'd SQLite? Needs validation.
design: See spec: .claude/plans/forge-resume-builder/refs/specs/2026-04-08-storage-abstraction-spike-design.md (Phase 4 section)
acceptance_criteria:
- DuckPgqAdapter implements StorageAdapter (read-only subset)
- EntityStore is read-only via ATTACH SQLite + DuckDB queries
- GraphStore uses CREATE PROPERTY GRAPH + SQL/PGQ pattern matching
- VectorStore not implemented (or delegates to DuckDB vss extension)
- AnalyticsStore uses DuckDB columnar engine for aggregation/scoring
- Validates ATTACH'd SQLite interop (critical unknown)
- Property graph DDL covers Forge schema (vertices + edges)
- Deployment exploration doc: embedded vs service vs CDN (CF R2)

title: Phase 3: GraphQLite Adapter
description:
Implement GraphQLite adapter for OSS/self-hosted Forge deployments. Cypher-based graph overlay on existing SQLite file — zero infra, single-file deployment. Pairs with sqlite-vec for vector search.

## Context
Phase 0 (adapter interface), Phase 1 (SQLite adapter), and Phase 2 (HelixDB adapter) are complete and merged. The StorageAdapter interface and EntityLifecycleManager are stable. This adapter delegates EntityStore to SQLite and adds GraphStore via Cypher queries through the GraphQLite SQLite extension.
design: See spec: .claude/plans/forge-resume-builder/refs/specs/2026-04-08-storage-abstraction-spike-design.md (Phase 3 section)
acceptance_criteria:
- GraphQLiteAdapter implements StorageAdapter interface
- EntityStore delegates to SQLite (relational tables are source of truth)
- GraphStore uses Cypher via SELECT cypher(...) over GraphQLite extension
- VectorStore delegates to sqlite-vec extension (if available) or brute-force fallback
- Extension loading works on macOS and Linux
- Graph sync mechanism mirrors FK/junction data as GraphQLite edges
- Integration tests pass against real GraphQLite extension
- Round-trip migration test: sqlite → graphqlite → sqlite

title: Resume compiler profile fetch refactor
description:
## Original Goal

The resume compiler at `packages/core/src/services/resume-compiler.ts` ~line 117 does 3 raw SQL queries to fetch the user profile, then manually JOINs address + URLs:

```ts
const profileRow = db.query('SELECT * FROM user_profile LIMIT 1').get()
const address = db.query('SELECT * FROM addresses WHERE id = ?').get(profileRow.address_id)
const urls = db.query('SELECT * FROM profile_urls WHERE profile_id = ? ORDER BY position').all(profileRow.id)
profile = { ...profileRow, address, urls }
```

This duplicates the exact logic in `ProfileService.fetchSingleton()`. Goal: eliminate the duplication.

## Current Context

**Compiler is sync, ProfileService is async.** `compileResumeIR(db, resumeId, elm?)` is a synchronous function. `ProfileService.getProfile()` returns `Promise<Result<UserProfile>>` (ELM is async). Can't call it without making the compiler async.

**Making the compiler async is high-churn.** ~70 test call sites + 5 production callers (ResumeService, ExportService) would need `await`. Callers are already async, but tests all call `compileResumeIR(db, resumeId)` synchronously.

**The compiler already has an optional `elm` param** but doesn't use it for profile fetching.

## Options Identified

**A) Full async conversion** — Make compiler async, use ProfileService.getProfile(). High churn (~70 test sites), but fully eliminates raw SQL for profile.

**B) Sync helper extraction** — Extract the 3 raw SQL queries into a `fetchProfileSync(db)` helper. Keeps compiler sync, reduces inline mess, but still duplicates ProfileService logic.

**C) Accept the duplication** — The raw SQL works, is tested, and is unlikely to change. The "refactor" may not be worth the churn. Revisit when the compiler gets a larger async rewrite (Phase 2 HelixDB work will force this anyway).

## Decision Needed

Have a conversation to decide which option (or a hybrid) is the right trade-off given the Phase 2 HelixDB work on the horizon.

title: Contacts: 'links' section auto-sets current contact as source
description:
When viewing a contact's detail page and adding a 'link' (relationship to another entity), the current contact should be automatically pre-populated as the source/from side of the link. Currently the user has to manually select the contact they're already looking at.

UX: on the contact detail page, the 'Add Link' action should pre-fill source=current_contact and only ask for the target entity + relationship type.

title: Normalize all notes into Notes table with FK links
description:
Several tables store raw 'notes' text inline (sources.notes, resumes.notes, organizations.notes, job_descriptions.notes, etc.). These should all be migrated to the existing Notes table with FK references via note_references.

Pattern: entity row has no 'notes' column. Instead, notes are created in the 'notes' table and linked via 'note_references(note_id, entity_type, entity_id)'. This is already the pattern for manually-created notes - extend it to cover all inline notes fields.

Migration: move existing inline notes text into notes table entries, create note_references, then drop the inline notes columns.

title: Extension: Forge server logging endpoint (POST /api/extension/log)
description: Extension sends serialized ExtensionError objects to POST /api/extension/log. Stored in extension_logs table. Gated by enableServerLogging config flag. WebUI page at /extension/logs for viewing. Enables remote debugging.

title: Extension: context menu 'Capture to Forge' on LinkedIn job pages
description:
Add a Chrome context menu entry that appears on LinkedIn job pages. Right-click → 'Capture to Forge' triggers the same jd.captureActive flow as the popup button.

**Implementation:**
- chrome.contextMenus.create() in background worker with documentUrlPatterns: ['*://*.linkedin.com/jobs/*']
- chrome.contextMenus.onClicked listener calls handleCaptureJob() (reuse existing handler)
- Cross-browser: contextMenus API works in Chrome + Firefox MV3
- No DOM manipulation, no content script changes needed
- Zero breakage risk from LinkedIn UI changes

**MVP follow-up (Option B):**
- Inject a sibling button next to LinkedIn's share button for better discoverability
- Requires MutationObserver for SPA navigation
- More fragile — needs resilient selector strategy
- File as separate bead when ready
close_reason: Subsumed by 3bp.23 (generic context menu covers LinkedIn + all job sites)

title: Extension: in-memory session cache for org resolution + URL dedup
description:
Add lightweight in-memory caching in the background service worker to avoid redundant API calls during a session.

**What to cache:**
- Org search results: Map<companyName, orgId> — same company name → same org within a session
- Org creation results: add to org cache after create
- URL dedup positive hits: Set<canonicalUrl> — known captured URLs return 'Already captured' instantly

**Implementation:**
- Simple Map/Set in service worker memory (not chrome.storage.local)
- Cleared automatically on service worker restart (correct TTL = session)
- ~2KB for 50 entries — negligible memory impact
- Cross-browser safe (Map/Set work in all browsers)

**What NOT to cache:**
- Health check (must reflect real-time state)
- Negative lookupByUrl results (JD might be created via webui between checks)
- Full org list (large, changes frequently)
- JD creation results (write operation, no re-read needed)

**Performance impact:**
- Second click on same page → instant 'Already captured' (no HTTP)
- Multiple JDs from same company → org resolution from cache (skip GET /api/organizations)
- Reduces 2-4 HTTP round trips to 0-1 for repeat operations

title: Extension: multiple/alternate locations on JD
description: Some JDs list multiple locations (e.g. 'Kirkland, WA; Sunnyvale, CA'). Currently only one location string is captured. Should support an array of locations. Ties into the Campus linkage feature.

title: Extension: work posture as separate field (Remote/Hybrid/On-site)
description: Remote/Hybrid/On-site should be a distinct 'posture' field on the JD, not conflated with location. If posture is 'Remote', that should also be set as a location value. Requires ExtractedJob schema change + LinkedIn plugin update to classify the workplace chip separately.

title: LinkedIn plugin: capture org LinkedIn URL during org creation
description: When creating a new org during capture, the LinkedIn page has the company's LinkedIn URL (e.g. linkedin.com/company/anthropic). Extract this and pass it to organizations.create({ linkedin_url }).
close_reason: Merged M2: org LinkedIn URL pass-through

title: LinkedIn plugin: extract salary from JD body text
description: The salary chip extraction (3bp.13) covers metadata chips. But some jobs have salary only in the description body text (e.g. '$147,000-$211,000'). The plugin should fall back to regex-matching salary patterns in the description if no chip is found.

title: LinkedIn plugin: extract external Apply link URL
description:
LinkedIn job posts often have an external 'Apply' button that goes through linkedin.com/safety/go/?url=<encoded_url>. The plugin should:

1. Find the Apply button/link on the job detail page
2. Extract the href (linkedin.com/safety/go/?url=...)
3. Decode the url query param to get the actual destination
4. Store as a new apply_url field on ExtractedJob

Example:
- Raw link: https://www.linkedin.com/safety/go/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fjob-boards.greenhouse.io%2Fanthropic%2Fjobs%2F4982193008
- Extracted: https://job-boards.greenhouse.io/anthropic/jobs/4982193008

Useful for: direct application links in JD records, future Greenhouse plugin, P5 dedup.
close_reason: Merged M2: apply URL extraction

title: LinkedIn plugin: salary range extraction truncates leading digits
description:
Target: https://www.linkedin.com/jobs/view/4322360108/
Got: 320,000—$405,000
Expected: 300,000—$405,000

The LinkedIn plugin's salary chip selector/parser is truncating the first character of the lower bound. Likely a CSS selector or text extraction issue in the salary parsing logic in packages/extension/src/plugin/plugins/linkedin.ts.
close_reason: Merged M2: salary chip truncation fix

title: Cross-entity taxonomy alignment — apply updated domains/archetypes/skills to all entries
description:
After completing the skills, domains, and archetypes audits, sweep all entities that reference them:
1. **Bullets**: Review domain assignments. Many bullets have incorrect or missing domains (e.g. Linkerd bullet tagged 'ai_ml' instead of 'devops').
2. **Perspectives**: Review archetype and domain assignments. Ensure they match the updated taxonomy.
3. **Bullet-skills links**: After rebuilding bullet_skills (see forge-0nf), verify skill assignments match the updated categories.
4. **Resume entries**: Check that resume skill selections align with the updated taxonomy.
5. **Gap discovery**: During this sweep, flag any new skills/domains/archetypes that should be added to the taxonomy.
Depends on: skills audit, domains audit, bullet_skills rebuild (forge-0nf).

title: Document R2 access key rotation procedure for forge-tf-state bucket
description: Per xk0z.21 spec Open Questions, R2 access key rotation is operationally distinct from passphrase rotation: it cannot be done atomically (revoke old → create new → push to 1Password → next workflow run), so there is a brief window during which CI cannot acquire the new credentials. Output: a half-page operational note documenting (a) when to rotate (annual or on suspected exposure), (b) the exact sequence of dashboard + 1Password steps, (c) how to verify the new key works before retiring the old one (run a manual workflow_dispatch on tf-plan-cf), (d) the maintenance-window cadence. Lands in docs/src/operations/ once that directory has its first occupant. Defer until first rotation is actually needed; document the procedure now while context is fresh.

title: TF state encryption passphrase rotation (annual cadence; first rotation 2027-04-28)
description: Per xk0z.21 spec Section 7, the state-encryption passphrase rotates annually or on suspected exposure. SOP: generate new passphrase, store as state-key-cf passphrase_v2 in 1Password, edit encryption.tf to add new key_provider/method as primary with old as fallback, PR + apply (re-encrypts state with new key), follow-up PR removes fallback + old key_provider. Apply same flow to terraform/github/ state. First scheduled rotation: 2027-04-28 (one year after spec landing). Defer until then unless a security incident warrants earlier rotation. Tracked here so the cadence does not silently drift past.

title: Docs: evaluate Astro Starlight (or alternative) for product docs after Rust rewrite of forge-server
description:
Today, `docs/` (the operator mdBook) is the only documentation surface in this repo, deployed to docs.resu.mx as a public site (per session decision 2026-04-28). MDBook fits the operator-handbook use case well.

When the Rust rewrite of forge-server lands (cf. forge-xk0z.5 hybrid JWT and the broader product migration), there will be product-side documentation needs that may not fit MDBook as cleanly:

- API reference (potentially generated from OpenAPI/Rust types)
- Versioned docs (multiple Rust API versions live concurrently)
- Embedded interactive examples (try-it-now widgets, OAuth playgrounds)
- i18n if forge ever localizes
- Search across both ops + product docs

## What to evaluate at that point

| Option | Fit |
|--------|-----|
| Stay on MDBook for everything | If product docs stay simple |
| Add Astro Starlight as a *second* docs surface (e.g., docs.resu.mx for product, ops-docs.resu.mx for ops) | If audiences diverge enough |
| Migrate everything to Astro Starlight | If consolidation > toolchain cost |
| Other Astro-based options (Astro + custom layout, Vitepress, Docusaurus, etc.) | If Starlight doesn't fit |

## Trigger

Open this bead when the Rust rewrite of forge-server is in progress and product-docs needs become concrete. Until then, MDBook stays the answer for ops docs.

## Out of scope

- Changing the ops docs surface today (MDBook is fine).
- Picking the Rust rewrite plan itself (separate epic).

title: Bullet-Skill Implicit Tagger Model
description:
Train a model that identifies latent/implicit skills in bullet text without explicit tagging. 'Managed production K8s clusters serving 10M req/day' should output [Kubernetes, Production Operations, High-Scale Systems, SRE] even without any being explicitly tagged.

## Why a Model

Current approach: skills are explicitly tagged on bullets by the user or extracted by LLM. Many skills are IMPLICIT in bullet text but never tagged. A dedicated model would:
- Read bullet text
- Output a ranked list of skills (from the skill graph) that the bullet demonstrates
- Confidence scores for each
- Works without LLM inference (small model, browser-compatible)

## Training Data

- Existing bullet → skill tag pairs in Forge (positive examples)
- LLM-generated labels on untagged bullets (silver labels, validated by human review)
- Skill graph provides the label space (output is skill graph node IDs)

## Architecture Options

- Multi-label classification on top of sentence embeddings
- Retrieval-based: embed bullet → HNSW match against skill embeddings → re-rank
- Hybrid: classification head for common skills + retrieval for rare skills

## Priority

P4 — after production. Current explicit tagging + LLM extraction is sufficient for MVP.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Model identifies 80%+ of human-tagged skills from bullet text alone
- [ ] Produces relevant implicit skills not explicitly tagged (validated by human review)
- [ ] Confidence scores correlate with human judgment
- [ ] Runs in browser via transformers.js
- [ ] Model weights < 30MB

title: Skill Embedding Model v2 (structured inputs)
description:
Train a custom skill embedding model that uses structured metadata (category, aliases, co-occurrence context, graph neighbors) as input features, not just skill name text. Produces higher-quality embeddings for the skill graph HNSW index.

## Why Custom Embeddings

Generic sentence embeddings (all-MiniLM-L6-v2) embed skill NAMES as text. But 'Python' the language and 'Python' the snake are identical as text. Structured inputs resolve this:
- Skill name + category + aliases + top co-occurring skills as input
- Model learns that 'Python [language, programming, data-science, machine-learning]' is different from 'Python [animal, reptile, snake]'

## Training Approach

- Contrastive learning on skill pairs from the curated skill graph
- Positive pairs: alias-of, child-of, related-to edges
- Hard negatives: skills with similar names but different categories
- Training data: curated skill graph (10k+ nodes with typed edges)

## Constraints

- Output must be compatible with HNSW index (fixed-dimension vectors)
- Must run in browser via transformers.js for inference
- Training is server-side (can use full GPU)
- Model weights < 30MB (smaller than generic MiniLM since domain-specific)

## Priority

P4 — after production. Generic MiniLM embeddings are sufficient for MVP. This improves quality at scale.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Custom model produces embeddings that cluster same-category skills more tightly than MiniLM
- [ ] Disambiguation: 'Python (language)' and 'Python (framework)' are separated in embedding space
- [ ] HNSW recall@10 improves over MiniLM baseline on skill matching benchmark
- [ ] Runs in browser via transformers.js
- [ ] Model weights < 30MB

title: JD Component Extraction Model (transformers.js)
description:
Train or fine-tune a small model for JD section identification that runs in-browser via transformers.js. Replaces current regex/heuristic section parsing with a learned model.

## What This Model Does

Given raw JD text, classify sections into: RoleResponsibilities, RoleReqsHard, RoleReqsSoft, RolePreferences, RoleDescriptionProse, RoleTeamDescription, RoleSalaryRange, RoleLearnMore, OrgAboutUs, OrgBenefits, OrgDisclaimers, etc.

## Why a Model (not just regex)

JD formats vary wildly across companies. Regex catches common patterns but fails on:
- Unlabeled sections (requirements listed without a 'Requirements' header)
- Non-standard headers ('What You Bring' vs 'Requirements')
- Mixed sections (benefits embedded in description)
- Bullet lists without section context

## Constraints

- Must run in browser via transformers.js (WASM/WebGPU)
- Must be small enough for reasonable browser download (< 50MB model weights)
- Must be fast enough for interactive use (< 1s per JD)
- Training data: existing JDs in Forge + public JD datasets

## Priority

P3 — after ALL production development is complete. Current heuristic parser (forge-3bp.21) is sufficient for MVP.

## Connects To

- forge-f3iq (JD Description automate section identification)
- forge-3bp.21 (Extension JD Structural Parser)
- forge-czgq (Extraction Pipeline — section-aware extraction strategy)

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Model trained on 500+ labeled JD sections
- [ ] Accuracy > 85% on held-out test set
- [ ] Runs in browser via transformers.js in < 1s per JD
- [ ] Model weights < 50MB
- [ ] Integrated into extraction pipeline as section pre-processor

title: Docker Hub publishing — public forge images
description: Push forge images to Docker Hub (docker.io/arustydev/forge) for public distribution. Requires GHCR pipeline working first. Add as secondary push target in CI workflow.

title: R2+: Migrate resume templates from LaTeX to Typst
description: Once the Rust port is stable (R1+), rewrite resume templates from LaTeX (XeTeX/tectonic) to Typst markup. Pure Rust, 10-50x faster compilation, 60-75% smaller binary, no C dependencies. Requires rewriting all .tex templates (sb2nov, etc.) into Typst format. See .claude/plans/forge-resume-builder/refs/specs/2026-04-23-latex-library-research.md for full comparison.

title: L3: Small model fallback for ambiguous sections (optional)
description:
For sections that L1+L2 classify with 'low' confidence, run through a small model for classification. Two options to evaluate:

Option A: Fine-tuned MiniLM/DistilBERT (~50MB ONNX) - fast, browser-compatible via ONNX Runtime Web (WASM). Needs training data (~500-1000 labeled JD sections).

Option B: Qwen2.5-0.5B via WebLLM (~300MB) - general purpose, no training data needed, but slower and larger. Uses WebGPU.

This is an enhancement layer - L1+L2 should handle 80-90% of JDs without it. Only build if the heuristic approach proves insufficient.

Decision gate: After L1+L2 are implemented, measure classification accuracy across 50+ real JDs. If accuracy is >90%, skip L3.
